evening, everyone, and welcome to the New

Siddhartha Mukherjee. [ Applause ] So they're here to talk
about Sid's new book, The Song of the Cell: An
Exploration of Medicine and our radical new
ability to manipulate cells. It is the third in
his exploration of what it means to be human. In 2011, he joined us here
in discussion at the Library of his magisterial
exploration of cancer, The Emperor of All
Maladies, and in 2016, came back to explore
the second, The Gene: An Intimate History, in 2016. We are so delighted and honored that Sid is back

tonight for being part of this important conversation. Now please join me in
welcoming Andrew Solomon and Siddhartha Mukherjee. [ Applause ] >> Andrew Solomon: Let me echo
those thanks to all of you for being here and to Tony for
that generous introduction. And let me just say that
most of you have not yet had an opportunity to read
Siddhartha's extraordinary book. But perhaps many of you
have had the opportunity to read the previous
extraordinary books. I always say that my primary
claim to literary fame is that I was the first
person to blurb The Emperor of All Maladies that I
then reviewed The Gene, and I'm pleased to
say that I'm quoted, in passing at least,
in this text. And part of what's so
astonishing about Sid's work is to have a real genius
in multiple areas that all intersect
so gracefully. In addition to personal charm
and dynamism, there is his work as a doctor and the
patients he treats. His extraordinary ability
to conjure those people as three-dimensional people and to see them fully
and compassionately. There is his ability as a writer
to use the English language with beauty and grace
and elegance. And there's additionally
his work as a researcher. There are whole long tranches
of this book that I looked at and thought, how on earth
could he be doing that research as well as that research
while writing his books? So it's a thrill and an honor
to be here on stage with you. This one, to me,
was in some ways, the most exciting of the three. Maybe just because it's the one
that I've most recently read. But in dealing with the cell, he
really gets at a beautiful song, at an aria in effect, about
what it means to look at life, what constitutes life, what
the boundaries are of life, what it is that we
now stand to do as we understand
life more deeply. And it melds as is his
want history and science and all kinds of other
disciplines in order to give a really full
and rounded picture. When I started in magazine
journalism, the wisdom was that it was boring to
write an all about story. You had to have an angle. But Sid Mukherjee has
made the all about story into an absolute centerpiece of
our understanding of humanity, of science, of life itself. So with that, I feel like
there are 1,000 directions in which one could begin. But I wanted to start,
actually, with the question of how you reconcile scientific
insight that you arrive at in a lab and the
process of writing. It's obvious that the science that you do feeds your
ability to write books. But I wondered whether
the writing of books also, in some ways, feeds
the work that you do as a doctor and as a researcher? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Well, first of all, thank you, Andrew and thank you to the
New York Public Library. Thank you to my family
and my wife and everyone else who's here. I write to think and I don't
think I'm the first person who's done that. The capacity to write allows me
to enter my own mind and places in my mind which would
otherwise be very hard to enter. So a lot of my writing
enables a kind of thinking. You know, these books
are landscapes. They are -- The Economist,
today, called it a tour d'horizon. And when you tour a landscape, I
think you begin to understand -- when you move above a landscape,
5 feet, 10 feet, 20 feet, 200 feet, you begin to
understand the contours of that landscape very
differently as opposed to when you're sitting
inside the weeds. And for every single book, my ability to understand
the contours of a landscape, the landscape has been
incredibly helpful in the sciences, because
it's allowed me to understand where I came from,
intellectually, and where we're going. Because you can't understand where you're going unless
you know where you came from. And so this idea of
writing to think -- I mean, I'm not comparing
myself to other great writers who think, but Darwin
did it famously. I'm always reminded of -- I don't know how
many people saw -- have seen the first time
Charles Darwin wrote down or created the -- what's
now considered the classic phylogenetic diagram, in
which he divided up the world, the evolutionary
world, into a tree. It's probably --
I love diagrams. Actually, there are diagrams
drawn by me in this book. But there's a lovely thing about
that diagram, because on top of that, after having
finished probably one of the most important diagrams
in the history of diagrams, he wrote on top of
that, I think. Because he wasn't absolutely
sure, but this idea of writing to think, I think
is very important and really motivates
a lot of the work. >> Andrew Solomon: And
when you talk about Darwin and about the way that
he used the progress of his own realization as a
means of narrating the insights at which he ultimately arrived, one of the things
that's striking here is that between passages, some of which are quite densely
scientific, accessible, because that's one of
your remarkable gifts, but densely scientific, you
have all of the historical stuff about how so and so was
born to an un-landed milkmen in Nebraska, or in Suffolk,
or wherever it was, made such and such a discovery,
and you give a lot of those personal details. How does all of that historical
information illuminate the insights about what we now know
about cells and cell biology? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: Well, I think that historical
information tells you where people came from and
what they were thinking about and why they were thinking
about those things. Robert Hooke is an
interesting example. He was born to an
indigent family. But because he was so brilliant,
went to Wadham College and became Robert Hooke. What's interesting about
Hooke, who actually is one of the first characters
we meet in this book, he actually was the first person
to visualize cells in plants. He didn't fully understand
the context, but he was the first
to visualize. And I call his intelligence,
I say it was phosphorescent and elastic like a rubber band
that glows when it stretches. It's a little bit of a
pun, because he, of course, Robert Hooke, discovered
elasticity. The laws of elasticity
are named after him. But he was a physicist,
an engineer, and he mastered optics,
mechanics, architecture. There's nothing that was
-- nothing was left behind. And so you need to understand
that person, I think, and their personhood to
understand them looking down a microscope and saying,
I'm going to start cutting up tissues and looking
in drops of water to try to find what's -- what
the microscopic world is. By the way, he also
owned a telescope. But while he was
looking at the skies, there's also microscopic
cosmos underneath. And you need to understand
that person. I'll give you one more
example of William Harvey. In some ways, very much
the opposite of Hooke. Didn't have a lot
of instruments. William Harvey discovered
circulation and he discovered the fact
that the blood circulates. Before that, a lot of
people claim that blood was like a heating -- was the
heating oil of the body and would just heat
all the other organs and then go into dead ends. He discovered that
it was circulatory and that was the basis of our
understanding of circulation, the thing that keeps us alive. And Harvey, unlike Hooke,
was not a microscopist. He had very few tools. And so his tools -- and
there's a nice illustration from his book. His tools were a tourniquet. He would pinch things. And he would take animals and he would take a little
venule and bleed them. And those were his three tools. So he was, I think, a person
of incredibly austere taste, both medically and
also personally. He had a -- he was
offered a massive house because he became the senior
professor at the hospital, but he preferred to live in
Ludgate, in a tiny house. And there's some connection,
always, between these people, their interiority, their
psychology, their desires, and their presence, that
informs their science. And that's why we
return to them. We make them -- that's why I try
to make them three-dimensional. Because if they're not three -- if you don't know that Darwin
had an almost spiritual crisis -- Darwin is not in
this book, by the way. But Darwin had a
spiritual crisis. You wouldn't know that that
spiritual crisis is what ultimately led him to the ship
and led him to the Galapagos and finally, you know, throughout the theory
of evolution. But you have to know about
that spiritual crisis, because you wouldn't
understand him, otherwise. You wouldn't understand
the book, otherwise. >> Andrew Solomon:
It's one of the things that works very powerfully in
this book and in the others, and it made me wish that
you were my science teacher or my son's science teacher, because those personal
narratives do actually explain the logic behind
the discoveries. And the memory of who discovered
what, when they discovered it, and how one discovery grew out of another makes much
more sense once it becomes a narrative of human dynamics. It's also striking
that you appear to have had personal
interaction with all of the most important scientists of the late 20th
and 21st centuries. There are moments when I
think, you met him too? How did you meet him? They're all there. So tell me how those personal
relationships that you built up with all of these
people at a Green Gordon, many others whom you catalog? How did those end
up informing you? Did you already have
those points of curiosity before
you met them? Or was it talking to
them that allowed you to move toward the
insights in the book? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: You
know, they're kind of random. It doesn't seem random
when they're in the book, but they're kind of random. Paul -- I think Ursula is here. Did I see Ursula? Hi Ursula. Ursula is Paul's wife. Paul and Ursula and Sarah and
I used to go to Maine together as part of a slightly mad camp,
which was very dear to us, run by a woman named Kippy
Stroud, who passed away, sadly. And I met Paul walking along
the beach and we became friends because Paul was
interested in everything. And I cooked him a meal. I think I made some kind of
lobster stew or something and he said, wrong career. As in, he wanted
me to be a cook. So anyway, so that's
how I met Paul. Paul Nurse who discovered
the way cells divide, I met a completely
different way. I didn't even know that. He and I were supposed to
be giving lectures together at a university in
Utrecht and by chance, our planes landed together. So they said, why don't you
both go in the same car? But it was a long ride from
Amsterdam and we were passing by and I started talking to him. And I, you know, chatted
with him for a while. And so that was a
complete coincidence. And he told me everything. He's very forthcoming and
he just told me everything. Who else comes to mind? There's some physicians here. Helen Mayberg, I think,
may be here somewhere. I sought her out, because I
was very interested in the work that she was doing and how it
began to change the way we think about putting in
electrodes into human brains and becoming new humans. So it's a real mixture. I sometimes seek them out
because I am very curious about why they're doing
what they're doing. Sometimes I meet them
because the airline happens to be landing at the same place
at the same time and sometimes, it's a windblown beach in Maine. >> Andrew Solomon: So
the next time that you're in a shared Uber, put
away your cell phone. You may have the
bestseller on your hands. You mentioned Helen
and you had talked about possibly wanting
to read a passage. And since the passage you
had mentioned involved Helen, though, you may have
chosen another passage in the meanwhile, why
don't you choose something and read aloud for. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Yeah, I'd love to read it. >> Andrew Solomon: And while
he's finding his place, I would just like to add that
the cooking really is very good. I left it off the
list of extraordinary and apparently irreconcilable
achievements. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
I'm still looking for it. So you can keep -- >> Andrew Solomon:
So I can keep going on about the food, the writing? I will, at some point,
ask you about metaphor. One of the things that
sparkles here as elsewhere is that there are an enormous
number of metaphors that make very difficult
sounding ideas feel very accessible, and they seem to
spring almost out of nowhere. So there's a constant refrain
of the way that certain cells in the immune system resemble
the beaks of different finches and a parallel with evolution. Talking about how you would
have a sharp-beaked finch who had certain abilities and
a sort of spoon-beaked finch who might have other abilities
and how the different functions of cells resemble them. There are descriptions
of great grandfather's. There are descriptions of the
way that parts of cells chew up other parts of cells. I think you've now found
your passage, but I can go on in this vein for
hours and hours. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: So
I won't read Helen's passage, just because I read
it at another reading. So I'll read a passage
called The Citizen Cell. Let me talk a little bit, before
I do that, about the structure of this book, because it's
a very unusual structure and it was -- it
took me a long time. And Nan Graham, my editor
and publisher, who we share, and I talked a lot about
how to structure this book. The structure of a book
is extraordinarily crucial and you know that as a writer. Of course, I know
that as a writer. But it is the first thing
that you figure out. There are two things, I think,
that are very, very important to figure out in writing, and
I call them structure and mood. You should know the mood of
a book before you know a book and you should know
the structure of a book before
you know a book. The rest of it follows. And I struggled with
the structure for the following reason. In writing nonfiction, you
always follow the golden rule, which is chronology
is your best friend. And in Emperor, chronology
was a great friend, because you could start
at, you know, with Atossa, the Greek Queen, who may or
may not have had breast cancer, and you end up in, you
know, 2016 or 2010. And it's just chronological. The Gene had a
semi-chronological structure, again, you begin with our
first conceptions of heredity. You then move on and come
to the discovery of DNA, et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. Cell biology is very
different and the history of the cell is very different. Because there is a
chronology that begins it, you find Leeuwenhoek
and Hooke therapists that we can talk about. But then the structure
becomes very complicated, because you can't say -- because
everyone is discovering things at the same time. And they're discovering
things at the same time and they're often, obviously,
collaborating with each other. But it would become --
because the cell itself is so functionally diverse. Your neuron looks nothing
like white cells in your body. It looks nothing like a
skin cell on your skin. It would become absurd
to go from the neuron to the skin chronologically. So I made a very bizarre and
somewhat radical decision that every chapter
would be a cell. So every chapter
then becomes a cell. And each chapter is
a mini-chronology. So each chapter is a full
history of that cell, of the discovery, of what
was thought about it, and how it changed over time. And so there's a
chronology inside it, but also it signifies -- each chapter signifies some
functional property of that cell that allows us to live. So it becomes a book of life. So every piece of it is -- has something to do with an
essential quality of life. Sentience, in the
case of your neurons. You know, it could be immunity in the case of your
immune cells. The carriage of metabolism
and carrying out of metabolism in the case of your liver cells,
et cetera, et cetera, et cetera. So it's a very uniquely
structured book. And I grew more and more
fond of the structure as I learned about it. Anyway, so I thought I would
read something that's -- I just read a piece
from Helen's book and it was just published
in The Guardian. So I would encourage
people to read that excerpt. But this is about the
heart and its own chapter, and it's called The Citizen
Cell and it's subtitled, The Benefits of Belonging. And the reason is --
to take a step back. The reason is that constructing,
you may think that -- again, we don't think
about it, typically, but constructing a pump
out of cells is not easy. You have -- a pump that doesn't
coordinate its motion is a wobbling balloon. And if that pump couldn't
coordinate its motion, we wouldn't be alive. Our circulation depends on it. Even primitive animals
have hearts, where there's a coordinated
motion that they pump together. And that citizenship, which
is the idea of belonging, is, of course, not just a
metaphor for the heart itself, but as a larger metaphor for
what the heart signifies, which is also belonging. So I thought that there
was something quite kind of lovely about it. It has a quick quote
to start with. This is from Elias Canetti, Crowds and Power,
very important book. And this whole idea of the
crowd, "The crowd, suddenly, where there was nothing
before was a mysterious and universal phenomenon. A few people may have
been standing together, 5, 10, or 12 not more. Nothing has been announced,
nothing is expected. Suddenly, everywhere
is black with people and more come streaming
from all sides, as though the streets
had only one direction and only one power." And this whole idea
of belonging together and working together becomes
very critical to the heart. So the way the heart
works separately is that unlike most
cells in our body, the heart cells are
all connected. They have channels
that connect them, such that when you spark a
single spark in one cell, the entire organ
contracts, because it's taken that nervous impulse and
moved it to the entire organ so that it can be one big lobe,
as they say, and it contracts. And then when those
ions leave the cell, the entire organ relaxes and
that's how you make a pump. You make a pump by belonging,
and every cell must belong. It belongs by physically
through channels, through molecular channels,
connecting with each other, such that the ions go in
together, make one single pump and then they leave together and
the thing relaxes, and so forth. So anyway, that's
about cell biology. But sometimes, as
I said, I don't -- I write about cell biology and
then I move on to other things. "The heart, of all organs in
the body, epitomizes belonging. We use the word belong to
signify attachment or love and the heart has been
the central signifier of that feeling for millennia. Of course, we know
now know that much of emotional life
sits in the brain. When you say my heart belongs
to you, you simplify the link between that organ
and attachment. As a child, my heart
belonged to my mother. My father was a distant
presence, reliable and gentle but reserved, somehow
unreachable. His mother, my grandmother
lived with us." This is in a book
about cell biology. [Brief laughter] "Scarred by
her move during partition, she kept to a room by
herself, cooked her own food, washed her own clothes, almost as if the house
was a temporary shelter that might be snatched
from her hands at any time. I have no memory
of her touching me. Her heart, as they
say, had been broken. My transition into manhood
marked a different relationship with my father. As a student at Stanford in
a world before cell phones and email, I began
to write to him. At first our letters were brief
and stilted, but over time, they grew longer and warmer. I began to understand
him in a new light. His loss of home
seemed familiar. In 1946, he had been
uprooted from his village when he was barely a teenager, stuffed into an overnight
ferry bound for Kolkata, a city on the edge of
a nervous breakdown. In the late '50s, he had moved
again as a young executive to Delhi, a city as culturally
and socially alien to young man from East Bengal as
the Frisbee-slinging, froyo guzzling, beer
ponging life of my California
dorm seems to me. I longed to be back home
even for a short while. One afternoon, I picked up my
mail and found a heavy packet. He'd surprised me
with tickets to return to Delhi in my first winter. It was a 16-hour flight
and I slept through it until the fog-choked lights
of the city came into view and the plane made that
elephant squeak sound of the wheel hatch opening
just before landing. Since that trip, I must have
flown to India four dozen times, but it's a sound that makes my
heart bound with a strange joy. The man at customs asked
me for a small bribe and I felt like hugging him. I was home. [Brief laughter] I can still
feel the boom of my heartbeat as I exited the airport. I could tell you about the
neural cascade I experienced. But while the stimulus was
triggered in the brain, the experienced first felt in my
heart, coming back, belonging." And then just a few pages
later, is exactly what I said. "But to imagine the heart
mechanically as a pump is to forget the central conundrum, how do you make a
pump out of cells? An uncoordinated pump is no
better than a wobbling balloon." And then we enter deep history. "In 1912 Alex Carrel,
a friend scientist at the Rockefeller Institute in
New York, cut a small fragment of the heart of an 18-day
old chick fetus and grew it in liquid culture
after first washing. And the culture grew
very extensively. And when he removed and
re-cultivated a piece, he found it's still
capable of pulsing. Carrel's experiment was
widely heralded as proof that organs could live and
function outside the body. But it signaled an
equally important idea, the cells of the heart,
cultivated outside the body, had an autonomous
capacity and a capacity of positive pulsates
together rhythmically, reminding me of Canetti's Crowd. Something intrinsic about
the cells made them capable of the pump-like action. It was a live demonstration
of belonging." So just that passage, I guess,
shows you some of the process by which I write, I make -- when
I write, I make no distinction between moving between
memoir and deep history, contemporary history, a
conversation in a car. Everything gets thrown
into the pot and it becomes a little
bit of a stone soup. >> Andrew Solomon: Well, it
becomes a little bit of a soup. I think, not a stone soup, but
a sort of rich one half-filled with wonders of every
description and without a stone. And the reversions to
narratives of India, historical narratives
India, tales and stories that are traditional in India, it makes a lovely
rhythm through the book. And there are other narratives
in it that are more likely to be familiar to an
American audience, and those are actually
quite illuminating. I was particularly taken with
the one of the metalsmith, which, I think, wasn't
in fact -- was it Indian? The metalsmith who makes the
armor that no spear can puncture and the spear that can
puncture any armor. And the question of how they
would interact dynamically, or might have been Chinese? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
It was Chinese. Yes. >> Andrew Solomon: But yes. But ventures into Asia. The question that seem to me
to be central to the book, and perhaps central to this
moment, is the question of the status of
science at a time when in the larger American
public there has been so much of an attack on the
notion of science itself. You have a chapter on
the pandemic and you talk about the ways that we've come
to understand it and you talk about the mysteries that
we cannot possibly unlock. And you give a wonderful
narrative of why SARS-CoV-2 and its many variants were more
successful at, if one wants to use the word successful,
at spreading than were many other
coronaviruses and many other potentially
pandemic-inducing attacks on the healthy ecosystem. But in the face of the pandemic,
one of the things that seemed to happen, as people refused
vaccines and refused masks and refused all of the rest
of these interventions, was that there was a sense that science was somehow
an egg-headed pursuit of coastal liberals and
was therefore irrelevant to the actual lived
experience of ordinary Americans who didn't have to bother
with all of that stuff. And part of what this book
accomplishes so beautifully, though I think it's unlikely
to reach those populations who are questioning [multiple
speakers] of science, is to argue very persuasively
for the clear, logical, complex, and highly personal way
that science has developed, and the way that each insight
has led to another insight, and by the end of the book,
an almost ecstatic description of all of what those
insights have already led to and could in turn lead to. So what do you make
of the politicization of the idea of science itself? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: Well,
I think it's very unfortunate. Obviously, that's the problem. I think that part of the reason
behind the politicization is that people have begun to think
of themselves as invincible, politically invincible, socially
invincible, and so forth. But it's, of course,
vulnerability that brings people
-- in some ways, vulnerability brings
people to science. And I mean vulnerability
in both ways. I mean, vulnerability in the
sense of not understanding. So you don't understand
the question. That's what brings scientists
to science, a young person to science, a kind of feeling
of desire to understand. But I also mean vulnerability
in terms of disease, illness. It is remarkable the --
SARS-CoV-2 was very unusual, because the people
who were the sickest and the most vulnerable
were elderly. The young felt invincible, invulnerable, at
least physically. And it was in the young
-- it was in that group, in particular, where the
anti-science sentiment really sort of caught fire and grew. Although, of course,
that's not just the group. The reason I bring
this up is that often, people come to science because
of the other vulnerability that we have, not the
vulnerability to ask questions when they're sick, when
they encounter disease, either in themselves
or in their parents. All of a sudden, this thing,
this egg-headed business that seems to be so far away
from them, hits them home. And people suddenly begin to
realize that this conversation that we're having --
not this conversation, but the scientific
conversation that we're having across generations
has very much to do with the alleviation
of suffering. And I think part of
the problem is for us to recognize what the
source of invincibility is and to simultaneously
recognize -- to simultaneously be
empathetic to the vulnerability. I don't hate anti-scientists. >> Andrew Solomon: I do. [Brief laughter] >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
I find them bizarre, I don't understand them. But I also think that that a
lot of that sentiment comes from a sentiment
of invincibility. And I think that they
become much, much more or much less anti-science when
they feel the vulnerability that inevitably will
come as they age and as they encounter
science as not just, you know, egg-headed people writing
equations, but doctors and nurses and others
doing their job, which is why I think medicine
is a very special science. Medicine is the one science that will absolutely
touch you in your life. There will be no time -- there
will be no one in this audience, I can guarantee, that will
not be touched by medicine and by that vulnerability. And I think as a writer, to
expose that vulnerability and the lack of invincibility,
the inability to answer questions --
we don't have answers to all questions and
it's frustrating. But being honest about it is -- I think helps the
deep politicization that we're going
through right now. And I think, as I said,
medicine is one way that we ultimately reach
the anti-science crowd. >> Andrew Solomon: I think
that's a very profound insight. I have written a lot about
mental illness, as you know, and I'm working on a book about
child suicide at the moment. And the people I've spoken
to who have been touched by it all express a
kind of incredulity at how little there
is out there about it. And I find, over and over
again, it's not merely that people haven't yet
arrived at vulnerability, it's that people don't want to
acknowledge the very possibility of vulnerability and
think that by denying or ignoring these problems, they somehow can
perpetuate the notion that they are not
vulnerable to the tragedies that they've seen
affecting other people. I think there's a strong
psychological mechanism in it as well. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: And,
you know, interestingly, I mean, maybe I'll ask you
a question, Andrew. I mean, it seems to me that
some of those people -- they come from so many
different walks of life. Some of those people when they
encounter the death of a child, maybe not all of them,
but some of them may begin to ask questions
about what happened. Potentially questions
about minds and brains and how brains develop, how social circumstances
influence the brain. And in beginning to
ask those questions, they don't go all the way. But I think it opens
a discussion, perhaps in their minds,
about the possibility that they can also
contribute to this -- to the egghead who
writes equations. And so I think the community
of science is also polarized. Scientists themselves
are polarized. And so I think that
a more welcoming way of dealing with anti-scientists. Not easy, but a more welcoming
way, maybe one way forward. >> Andrew Solomon:
I think that's true. And I think part of what strikes
me about this book as indeed about the past ones, in the
first one, I sort of thought, well, here's Siddhartha
Mukherjee. He's a very accomplished
oncologist and he knows a great
deal about cancer. And it was an enormously
broad book, because cancer is an
enormously broad topic. But it was still
effectively about an illness that probably affects almost
everyone directly or indirectly, but that nonetheless
remains quite specific. The Gene seemed to open
this up further and to talk about the whole nature of
how people are constructed and how we've all
come to be ourselves. I think cell biology and
part of what's revelatory about reading this, has roots that go back a very,
very long way. But essentially, cell biology,
as we know it, is far newer than any of the other things
that you have been looking at. And actually, in many
instances, shockingly new. Things that I learned
in ninth grade, which was now quite some time
ago, but not so long ago, that I hadn't realized had been
discovered only a few years before I got to ninth grade. And so one of the things
that this book has, which The Gene also had,
but which this book has, in particular, is a kind
of shimmering notion of where this will lead
us, which you expressed with some reservations about
what it means to enter any form of post-human future, but
which you nonetheless talk about as being the basis
for our expanding insights into human life and
indeed, all life. And the connectedness of all
life and the relationship between the basic science and
the clinical applications, which can often be so long and
so slow, seems here to progress with unusual speed and clarity. And so I wonder how you
contrast thinking about The Cell with thinking about The Gene? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: Well,
in some ways, the books are in a little bit of
a mumbo jumbo order. If I had to read the books,
I'll read The Gene first, then The Cell, and then the
Emperor of All Maladies. Because the gene is a unit of
information, the basic unit of information, but the cell
is the basic unit of life. What people often don't realize
is that genes are in DNA, obviously, except
for some viruses. But genes are in DNA, but that
molecule of DNA is lifeless. It is a molecule. It's a chemical. If you eat it, it'll go
right through your system. I was outside a Botox clinic
in Brazil, not getting Botox. [Laughter] Walking, as one
does outside a Botox clinic in Brazil. >> Andrew Solomon:
Give it a year or two. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Exactly. And it was called
The Life Clinic and there was a picture of DNA. But of course, that had
nothing to do with anything. They were not injecting DNA. But I realized that
this has become an icon. The DNA has become an icon of
life and yet it's lifeless. It's the cell that
enlivens the gene. It's the cell that makes
the gene come alive. And in fact, if we really speak
about life and the properties of life, we have to speak
about the cell and we have to speak about, you
know, what the cell does and what the cell can do. The second question that
you asked implicitly, is about the post-human world. And I make a very
important distinction in that post-human world and I
want that distinction to remain as one of the big
distinctions that I talk about. I make the distinction
between disease and desire. Disease, of course, has
to do with suffering, desire has to do
with augmentation. And for the very long -- for a
very long time, these two words, disease and desire, lived
in different universes, partly because we couldn't
do very much scientifically or otherwise. But over time, these words
have started coming together in some ways that are
fantastic, in some ways that are less fantastic. And I will give you one
example, which is kind of an interesting example. When the first IVF baby,
Louise Brown, was born, many obstetricians and
many bioethicists said that infertility
is not a disease. Human beings have been living
with infertility forever. So why define it as a disease? And others, not everyone, argued that there's an
aching to have a child. Not for everyone, but for
some, there was an aching to have a child and
they were suffering. And there was suffering
associated with having a child. Now we completely
assume, not for everyone, but for some people, that that
aching is a form of suffering. And hundreds of thousands of
babies have been born with IVF. Should we, as you ask,
in your previous book, extend those principles
to people with extreme short stature? Should we extend those -- that
blurring of disease and desire to people who are deaf? People who are blind
or partially blind? I think these questions are
becoming increasingly urgent, as I conclude at the end of
my book, as the two worlds of genetic engineering and
cellular therapy come together. And it's really important
to know that they have to come together. A gene put into the wrong place,
a wrong cell, and the wrong part of the body at the wrong
time is a chemical. It is the confluence of cellular
engineering and gene therapy which is bringing disease
and desire into a place that we don't know,
a very unknown place. And the only way that the book
proposal to resolve that is to live outside or to bring
yourself outside the world of science. Science can give you facts. It can provide information,
it can provide manipulation, but it cannot provide
a framework to understand the distinction
between these two words which have inhabited our
vocabulary from the beginning of human life 200-odd
thousand years ago. >> Andrew Solomon: I could go
on asking questions for hours, but apparently, some of the rest
of you wish to do so as well. So let me move on to some of the audience questions
that have come up. Here's one. Do you think it's possible to
apply our evolving understanding of cellular mechanisms and
function toward an understanding of the nature of consciousness
itself in both human and other forms of life? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Tough question. I think there are
strong attempts to understand consciousness
through science. It will require more
than just cell biology. We know how a neuron works. We know how circuits
of neurons work. We can manipulate
circuits of neurons, but that doesn't
equal consciousness. I think we will require tools
from computational biology, from computation, from what is
called artificial intelligence to create a simulacrum of
what we call intelligence. The problem is that it's going
be very difficult to compare that simulacrum of intelligence
to the actual process that goes on to create sentience
and consciousness and certainly it will
be, I think, impossible. So I think we'll
get somewhere there. I am doubtful that we will get
to using these kinds of models. I'm doubtful in conjunction
with cell biology, I'm doubtful that we will get to what I
consider the more profound forms of conscious thinking, which
might include things like love and belonging and
desire and compassion. I think we'll lie
outside the realms of at least what
we have right now. We'll probably need to invent
a new science to create, again, simulacra of those. And even then, they
will remain simulacra. Because our capacity to understand what
is actually happening in the brain will
lie outside it. But, you know, I was only
briefly a neuroscientist. I was a neuroscientist for
a while, but only briefly. So I think this question is a
little bit above my pay grade. >> Andrew Solomon:
[Brief laughter] But I think it's actually
part of what you do accomplish in the book, which is not only
to describe what science can do, but to describe the
-- I don't know. The words are also troubled. But the mystical, or spiritual,
or unknowable, at least, dimension of human experience
that it can't address. Here's one. Do you think that
life/sentience is a property of individual cells or
an emergent property of an aggregation of cells? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Well, I'm almost certain that sentience is a property
of aggregations of cells and I'm almost certain
that life is the property of an individual cell. I think sentience -- I
think cells have function. They have agency, but
that agency is linked to their function. So when a white blood cell
goes to capture an infection or when a neuron fires,
that is number one, living, and number two, that has agency. But that agency should
not be confused with things like sentience. Agency is not sentience. Sentience is a much more
complex characteristic. And so I think that
sentience usually appears -- the way we understand sentience,
again, in common language, usually appears in
multicellular beings. Although, as I said,
if you were to -- you could define
agency as sentience, but that's not how we
-- in common parlance, how we define sentience. >> Andrew Solomon: All right. So it becomes partly
a linguistic question? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: Yes. >> Andrew Solomon: And connects,
I think, to your description, there's a very compelling
description, which I suggested you
read and then you chose to read something else, of your
own experience with depression and what that was like and how
it changed your perspective. How would you say it shifted
your understanding of neurons and consciousness and their
complex interrelatedness? That and of course,
the work of scientists, including Helen Mayberg, who
I think is here this evening. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Well, you know, you have to hold a duality when
you think about mental illness. You have to hold the
duality that on one hand, that's an organic property
of cellular dysfunction. I go back to Rudolf Virchow, who makes a very
important statement, a very audacious
statement as a young man, adding the last two
principles of cell biology. The first is that all normal
physiology is the consequence of cellular physiology. No one had dared
to say that before. And if you really
think about it, it really changes the
conception of the human body. And the second sentence
is just its converse. All illness is cellular
dysfunction. Now, what he's not
-- he's not denying that environments
can impinge on cells, as they do in mental illness,
or that sometimes it can happen without the environment
impinging on cells. But ultimately, when all of
the factors, social, economic, environmental, parental, et
cetera, are taken into account, ultimately, it involves
a dysfunction of cells. That statement is
profoundly radical, because it reconceives the
entire notion of illness and it also allows us to, as
physicians, to tackle illness in a way that we
can tackle illness. That is not to say that
we should not be -- in other words, it is to say that we should be equally
attentive and are attentive to all the milieu that's causing
that cellular dysfunction. But of course, we can't
solve that milieu. I can't solve, at
least as a physician, cannot solve the crises that
precipitate mental illness. But someone like Helen or
others can find the circuit in the brain where -- which
may be responsible, ultimately, for the behavioral and cognitive
and the feeling as it were. >> Andrew Solomon: Effective. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Effective emotional being and intervene on that circuit
by putting in tiny electrodes, re-stimulating it, and in fact,
being successful at, at least, ameliorating many cases of
recalcitrant depression. Now, it's important to remember
that what I'm not saying is, I'm not saying depression
is caused by those -- that neural circuit. What I am saying is that
there could be 1,000 reasons that could be. As I said, there's a milieu. But what I am saying
is that as someone who experienced depression
myself, you have to hold both
the ideas in your head, that there is an organic
cause and there can be a cause that comes from the milieu. We, as physicians,
can't, some of us try, but can't intervene
with the milieu. Certainly psychotherapists
do, talk therapists do. But we can and are increasingly
able to intervene on the, what I call the least
final cause. You can't prevent -- as I said,
you may or may not be able to prevent that milieu, but you
can potentially intervene the least final cause. And there's a dichotomy there. It is not -- I'm not saying
this without some trepidation. Because in the wrong
hands, you begin to believe that the organic cause
is the ultimate cause and you confuse the
organic cause. So you have to hold the duality
in your hands at the same time, that there could be a
least common pathway which you can intervene on, but
you would prefer, of course, to change the milieu that caused
that least common pathway. >> Andrew Solomon: I think it's
the conflation of treatment and etiology, that you
might begin to feel better when your serotonin
levels go up. But that doesn't mean the
reason you felt bad was because your serotonin
levels were low. Any more than a house
fire that can be put out with water indicates
that your house caught fire because it should have
had more water in it. [Brief laughter] Here's a
question that I rather like. How do we improve medical
education in terms of language to tap into non-scientifically
curious children and youth? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: I think
language is really obviously very important to me
and important to you. I think the key is to avoid
jargon and to convince people of how -- of the
emotional density that the language carries. I think people think about medical languages being
jargonistic and useless. But there's an incredible
amount of emotional density that a word carries in medicine. When you're talking to
someone, every word -- and I do this for a living. When you're talking to someone, every word carries --
is made out of lead. And it falls like a drop of lead into that person's
consciousness. If you allow children who are
scientific or non-scientific to realize that emotional
density and to own it, I think we would vastly
improve medical education. >> Andrew Solomon: I would add,
just looking at my own children, that I think often, there is
a tendency to treat science as an accumulation of facts. And of course, science
involves a great many facts and it's useful to know them. But if you don't get at
what you've just described, the emotional content and
the larger significance that those facts point toward,
they're not very interesting and learning them
becomes just an exhausting and repetitive labor. If you're able to
come back to the point that you clearly are able
to reach in communicating with your patients, but also
in this book in communicating with the world, of showing that these facts
actually are significant. Because with them, you can
perhaps change your experience of the world, your
ability to stay alive, and so on and so forth. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Absolutely. I mean, that's the whole point. That is the emotional density
that's carried by science. I think that -- and
people lose it. People say science is facts. Of course, it's not facts. It's adventure. It's exhilarating. I often tell people -- I often
tell my graduate students, you know, how they're doing
in the middle of a project and I'll say, at the
end of this project, you will own a slice
of the universe. And it sends a chill
up my spine. You will own a slice
of the universe and no one else will own it. It will be yours forever. And that slice of universe, that slice of the
cosmos will be something that you completely own. And I think -- I hope
that inspires them. >> Andrew Solomon: I'm
going to end, because sadly, we're at the end,
with a question that seems appropriate
for ending with. Someone has written in, which
cell is your personal favorite? [ Laughter ] >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
All right. It's a tough choice. I like so many of them. [Brief laughter] You know,
I'll give you an example. I particularly like T-cells, and that's because I've
studied them most intensely. I was telling you
behind in the room that the proudest thing
I've done in my life is to create T-cell treatments
for the first time in India. So we've treated
10 children so far. It's not available. These are children who would die from refractory relapsed
leukemia and they couldn't afford it. In this country, they
can't afford it, of course. It costs about $1 million if you
take in all the other therapy. We're offering it to them
for free for now in trial and at a radically
slashed price. But T-cells are what are
used to kill the cancer. So we extract the
T-cells from their body. We genetically modify
them, weaponize them, grow them up in these dishes in
a completely sterile factory. You have to enter
with a hazmat suit. And we then give them
back to the children. It's very toxic therapy, so
there's doctors that monitor it. But it is, I think, the
proudest moment of my life when I saw the first
child cured. It's hard to describe that joy. I think it's impossible
to describe that joy. But that child is
walking around, went home and left me a picture
of an elephant. >> Andrew Solomon: [Brief
laughter] Well, that, again, is part of what's
so very impressive and astonishing about the book. First of all, I certainly knew
many people who had HIV or AIDS and who struggled with them. And I had endless
conversations with people about their T-cell counts without ever understanding
really what a T-cell did. And reading this book,
among its many, many gifts, gave me that knowledge which has
really allowed me to reassess, in retrospect, the
experiences of beloved friends, some still alive and
some no longer alive. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Well, sorry about that. >> Andrew Solomon: But the
book, I think, is also, as you've just described,
it's infused with a great deal of hope. I think someone said
about The Gene, that it reads like a thriller. And there are moments in
this in which we're sort of moving toward
understanding what happened in a particular instance or
a particular circumstance that are quite thrillery. But I think the overall message
of the book it feels to me, as riveting and as electrifying
as a thriller, is to say, we came to this insight,
this insight, this insight, and perhaps, sickle cell anemia
can be brought under control, perhaps these children
don't have to die anymore. And you write about them with such intimacy
and such compassion. And I think it's an
extraordinary achievement to be able to do that while at
the same time, teaching so much that we might not know. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Thank you very much. >> Andrew Solomon:
Thank you all. [ Applause ]

Alan Townsend's lab sat atop a steep hill at The Institute for molecular medicine at the edge of Oxford University in the fall of 93 when I arrived at Oxford to study it with Alan as a graduate student in Immunology the mysteries of T-cell function were still being deciphered The Institute was a modernist steel and glass building the security guard at the front desk a woman with a thick Welsh accent which you could probably do checked IDs before letting you in without the right card she would refuse to let you enter two years passed with me fumbling through my pocket for the card until I finally got the courage to confront her I had been there every day for 24 straight months didn't she recognize me by my face she looked at me stonly I'm just doing my job her job I suppose was to detect Intruders as if I might well have been James Bond who'd driven up the hill in an Aston Martin on a top secret mission to feed my T-cell cultures at night in retrospect I've grown to appreciate her diligence she had internalized immunity I'm going to skip forward a little bit an Italian postdoc Vincenzo chirundulo was my direct mentor Enzo was short garrulous and effervescent and yet during my first weeks in the lab he ignored me entirely he rushed around the lab navigating his way past me as if I was some irritating piece of lab equipment that someone had offloaded in the wrong place he was finishing a research paper and teaching a freshly arrived graduate student the details of Immunology seemed hardly worth his time one aspect of Enzo's project involved manufacturing viruses to infect Mouse in human cells to expand the virus that is to make more viral particles so that he could do this experiment you had to infect a layer of cells and then you extracted the virus by placing the whole culture in a tube and freezing and throwing it exactly three times it was a procedure that acquired precision and patience without freeze throwing you couldn't release enough but if you overdid the process you might kill the virus entirely in the entire experiment would go to waste one morning soon after I arrived I found Enzo fussing over one such tube a research technician also Italian had made a prep but she had left for a vacation Enzo had no idea whether the virus had been extracted or whether the tube had been left without the process it was a tense moment a low viral count and the whole experiment critical to the paper would go down the drain he cussed under his breath in Italian Cavallo by the way I was in Kolkata and someone came and asked me well what other cuss words do you know in Italian and I told him several others anyway I asked Enzo if I could look at the tube and he handed it to me and on its bottom in barely visible ink I saw that the technician had scribbled the letters c s c s c s what's the word for freeze in Italian I asked him conjulari Enzo replied and for thought so that's what the technician had written I I thought freeze thaw freeze Thor freeze thought except in a kind of Morse code cs cscs three times each Enzo regarded me sharply perhaps I wasn't a waste of time after all he finished his experiment and asked me if I wanted a coffee and he made two cups something had thought between us we'll move a little bit ahead in early 2019 I learned that Enzo had been diagnosed with Advanced lung cancer the news of his illness numbed me with shock I couldn't bring myself to call him for days a weak past perhaps too until I finally dialed his number from New York he picked up immediately he was matter of fact about his condition perhaps those T cells whose inner Mysteries he had spent his life uncovering would find a way to fight his cancer as Alan Townsend my mentor wrote about Enzo in the in a journal one hears the phrase fight with cancer but this description is a pale shadow of the intense personal grinding immunological battle he waged with the rebellious cells that challenged him he did this unperturbed never missing a seminar always available to students and colleagues it was a demonstration of supreme courage in 2020 a few weeks before I was about to visit Oxford to give a talk I found out that Enzo had died I canceled my trip that evening I sat in the lab silently recalling my mentor my friend holding back the memories until they had hardened I felt dazed and then desiccated congealed in Gloom it was only then hours later that the grief burst in me in wave upon liquid wave Enzo it's conjulari thank you ladies and gentlemen will be signing books in the tent afterwards I - Good afternoon and welcome. I'm Carol Christ. I'm the Chancellor of Berkeley and it's a delight to welcome you to this event this afternoon. You're here to hear a
conversation which has the title The Future of Humans, Gene Editing and the Unthinkable
Power to Control Evolution. That title, to me, recalled
the 200th anniversary of the publication of Mary
Shelley's Frankenstein in 1818. Frankenstein is the prototypical
text of the human ability to create new life, and it's
really a prescient text, particularly at the moment
when scientists have the power to manipulate the blueprint of life. This is a bold topic, and
a perfect way to kick off Berkeley's sesquicentennial, which we're celebrating with
the official motto Fiat Lux, or Let There Be Light. This motto inscribed
in the university seal and on the five-pointed
star that adorns Sather Gate reminds us that it's our
duty to create new knowledge and bring it to light, to illuminate solutions for
the world's greatest problems, and find solutions for
bettering the human condition. As we celebrate 150 years
of light at Berkeley, and we anticipate 150 years
of light in the future, the discovery of CRISPR
by Jennifer Doudna, and the global impact that this will have comes immediately to mind. Few breakthroughs in science have had the kind of sweeping, immediate
impact that CRISPR has, bringing monumental change to the way scientists approach key questions of life, and holding unlimited
promise for the future. It's exhilarating to imagine
the transformations we'll see as the result of this
technology in the coming years. Our special guest Dr.
Mukherjee is an oncologist at Columbia University
and Pulitzer Prize winner for his book, The Emperor of All Maladies, A Biography of Cancer. His most recent book The
Gene and Intimate History chronicles the history of the gene and what becomes of humanity when we can read and edit
our own genetic information. A Rhodes scholar, he has earned degrees from Stanford University,
the University of Oxford, and Harvard Medical School. Dr. Jennifer Doudna has been a member of the Berkeley faculty since 2002, and is the founding executive director of the Innovative Genomics Institute, a joint endeavor of UC Berkeley and UCSF, and I'd like particularly to welcome UCSF's Chancellor Sam
Hawgood to this program. (audience applauds) Since her days as a
graduate student and postdoc at Harvard and the University of Colorado, Jennifer has always been
interested in structural biology and the biochemistry of RNA. In 2012, she and her colleague
Emmanuelle Charpentier discovered CRISPR, and
that's a game changer. It holds potential to
cure genetic diseases, overcome climate change, and
address global food security, among many other
transformational applications. Dr. Doudna's contributions,
her light, are significant and worthy of celebration. Please welcome Jennifer
Doudna and Dr. Mukherjee. (audience applauds) - Well, thank you, Chancellor Christ, for that very warm introduction, and thank you to all of
you for coming today. We're really looking forward
to this conversation. We will talk for about 45 minutes and then we'd like to open
up the floor to questions to answer thoughts and ideas and give you a chance
to share those with us. I'd like to start off by pointing out that humanity has always had
a desire for improvement. Cars, improved transportation. Penicillin, improved health. The internet improved the
spread of information, or misinformation, in some cases. Now it appears that we're
entering a new stage of improvement, artificial intelligence, to improve efficiency. Self-driving cars to improve safety, and the improvement of our own bodies. So we have to truly ask
ourselves, what are we improving? Do we need improving? And even, what is improvement? So gene editing technologies
and cancer therapies are forcing us to look at these questions and also to look at ourselves. Cancer, for example, is a natural
and common cause of death. Genetic diseases are too numerous to count and a natural result of our being human. A new wave of biomedical
advances are giving us the ability to push back cancer
and erase genetic diseases, but at what cost? So I'd like to start this
conversation by asking you, Dr. Mukherjee, are we transitioning from natural to unnatural, or is this just the next
step in human evolution? - So, again, I think the
question of what natural and unnatural is or are has
been really brought about in the last decade or so
in a way with an urgency that really didn't exist before. Oscar Wilde famously said, "Being natural is sometimes just a pose." And the question is, how are we posing? What pose are we on? I do think that the
capacity to change something as elemental as our own DNA,
even if it's in somatic cells, certainly in stem cells and
certainly in embryonic cells, really raises the question
about what we're doing with our own evolution. These are unprecedented technologies because they allow us to hold
the horns of our own destiny in some ways, even though
we understand destiny is more than DNA, but a powerful element
of destiny is in DNA. You gave the example of
cancer and genetic diseases. So really the question of what is natural and what is unnatural,
the boundaries, I think, have been redrawn or
are being redrawn today. My own thoughts about this, really, are the thoughts of a physician. That's my brain. I have a physician's brain. And that is that as we do this, as we enter this arena
of new technologies, it seems to me that there are, and I talk a little bit
about this in The Gene, that there are a triangle of ideas, which we should keep in focus. The first one is when we intervene, when we're intervening on human genetics, is there extraordinary suffering involved? You and I can then open
up a debate saying, well, what is extraordinary suffering? But at least there's a sense, there's a hard line that we draw and say that when we move forward that that remains a
certain line in the sand, that we invade on what is
natural versus unnatural only when we think that there's extraordinary
suffering involved. In a second I'll tell
you that there are people who don't buy this argument. We'll talk about that in a second, but that's one line of the triangle. The second line of the triangle
is loosely I've called it, in the book, I've called it penetrance. The idea is, we can call it certainty, that when we make genetic changes, when we do tamper with evolution, particularly with our own evolution, that we have a strong degree of certainty that there's a powerful relationship between the gene and the phenotype, or the ultimate manifestation
that's involved. So things that we're uncertain about, things that have effects
that we don't understand, cascading effects, pleiotropic effects, we probably should avoid since, obviously, we don't want to intervene on things that we cannot ultimately control. And the third line of the triangle is, I've called it, justifiable choice. People have used different words for it, and that is that when we intervene between what is unnatural
and what is natural, that the intervention
bears some justification, that we can justify it, and that it's not done by state mandate, that individual choice is involved. People are able to do it or
not able as they should choose. Now, it doesn't take an
extraordinary complicated bit of thinking to realize that each of these is fuzzy territory. Who defines extraordinary suffering? Who mandates choice? If our cultures push us towards
manipulating our children, is that really a choice? Even if it's a passive choice, does it really become a choice? - [Jennifer] And, by the
way, different cultures might make different choices. - For instance, exactly, and you know better than anyone else, the rules that apply for
interventions on embryos in China are not the rules that
apply for intervention in the United States and other cultures. So I fully understand this
is complicated territory, but as we move forward, if
we don't draw some stakes, this is going to become, I think, even more complicated than it is. So it is absolutely true

you've written about this. So tell us about the evolution. - Yeah, I guess I started

you were sort of messing with something that maybe

appreciate that in many cases, a gene, and you know this
better than anyone probably, but a gene that we think

be sure that something we're altering might not
have unintended consequences? And that would have consequences
in not only a person, but all of their kids,
and their kids' kids, and it seemed kind of a profound thing. But what's happened over

always just been working on molecules rather

over the last few years is I've been coming into

for me these days, but I got an email from
a woman who explained that her beautiful, young
boy has a genetic disease that had just been diagnosed, and she sent me a picture
of this little baby in his little carrier. He's cute, and it just broke my heart. It really broke my heart and
I guess I just found myself recognizing that when
there is severe suffering and we have an opportunity to change that, it may be worth doing. - Well, you know, one line
that philosophers have tried to draw is between
emancipation and enhancement, and they have tried to draw a strong line. Of course, in real practice,
once you practice medicine, those lines also become fuzzy. What is emancipation for one person can be enhancement for the other. The extremes are quite obvious. It's very easy to think about extremes. Manipulating genes for cosmetic purposes seems very obviously far outside the realm of what we want to do, but on the other hand,
there are certainly examples where the conflicts are more real. The question that occurs to me, I'm gonna frame the question, and then maybe subdivide it a little bit. One question that occurred to me while I was thinking about
this is we're always, in medicine, straddling the line between treating and curing. But now we are beginning

crops and plants and pests. The second is somatic gene editing, so particularly things
like, I'm a transplanter, so in stem cells, blood stem cells, and other kinds of stem cells. And the third, of course, is gene editing in either sperm or eggs or embryos. So tell us about what you
think the prospects are in these three categories,
and is it reasonable? What is reasonable and what's unreasonable maybe in the three categories? Or pick one of them which
is most provocative for you. - Well, I think you're
touching on an important point, and that is that gene
editing is used now widely sort of across the biosphere,
I guess you could say. It's really being used in plants, animals. It's enhancing the pace of research. It's opening up organisms
for study that in the past really could not be investigated
at the genetic level. So it's a very exciting
time, really, as a biologist, I think, to be living in the midst of this transformational
opportunity with new techologies. And it's not just gene editing. It's other things, too, of
course, that are converging to give us ways that we
can manipulate organisms that were unthinkable in the recent past. So just having the ability
to sequence the entire genome and all the DNA in the cell of an organism and start understanding
what that code means, how it dictates what an organism
becomes is really profound. So just to quickly touch
on those three areas. So if we think about using
gene editing in organisms other than humans, clearly
this is going to have a big impact on humanity, right? Because it'll allow us to
change plants, for example, to be more nutritious, or
to grow in environments where they otherwise wouldn't thrive. - [Dr. Mukherjee] Mosquitoes. - Get rid of mosquitoes, maybe. Maybe, or at least control
spread of disease by insects. In terms of editing what you
referred to as somatic cells, and just so that everyone's
on the same page with that, that term just means
that we're editing cells that are not germline cells, meaning they're not cells that
are eggs or sperm or embryos. So they don't cause heritable changes in future generations of organisms, but this could still
be incredibly impactful for the clinical
application of gene editing, and the type of research
that you do, for example, being able to edit,
let's say, immune cells so that they're more
effective at targeting cancer would be amazing if we can do that. Editing blood cells so
they no longer carry the disease-causing gene
for sickle cell anemia would be incredible. And there's many, many
other examples like that. Then the third category is the one that is more kind of ethically
fraught, or could be, which is, should it be okay or is it okay to make changes in the germline of humans. Believe me, scientists
are already doing this in the germlines of
other kinds of organisms quite routinely, but in humans, not yet. And should we go there? And I personally think
that it's going to happen and it's partly gonna
happen because of the fact that different cultures view
this question differently, and I think we're seeing
already in the scientific world that the pace of research, at
least, on human embryo editing is proceeding differently
in different countries. - So, just a quick
follow-up on that question, and then we can move on. How long 'til the first transgenic human? - Um, oh dear, I'm on the spot here. (audience laughs) I don't know, is the short answer. I think that, again, so
that everyone out there sort of understands what's happening. I mean, we saw, just
in the last six months, there have been two very
prominent publications in scientific journals from
highly respected research labs, one here in the United States, and one in the United
Kingdom, who both showed that you could use gene
editing in viable human embryos to change, to alter the genome, to make a very precise change to a particular gene in these embryos. - [Dr. Mukherjee] There's
been some question about-- - There's been questions about the details of how that's working, but I think the overarching
thing that I take from that is that this research is proceeding, and I think the pace will accelerate. I recently heard from a colleague in China who is coming to visit me
here at Berkeley next week who wants to give me an
update on his own research in human embryos. So it's moving forward. I can't tell you when I
think it'll be actually used to create a CRISPR baby, for example, but I think we're seeing
steady progression in that direction. - I mean, just to give you one example, I work with blood stem
cells and immune cells. That's mostly why I'm a
biologist, leukemia doctor. As of last week, we were just
talking about this earlier on, as of last week, in cord blood stem cells, we are getting gene editing successfully in 90% of the stem cells. In fact, we've gotten to a
place where we're wondering if we should even sort these cells because the efficiency of sorting is getting to the efficiency of editing. So basically I suspect
that the blood system is open for business. The entire blood system
is open for business. There's many, many questions,
off target effects, whether we will get weird
leukemias that arise because we've made an
edit in the wrong place, but essentially speaking, it's staggering. We couldn't do this six months ago. - So this is a very important point. In the field of this technology and all the things that
are being done with it are moving forward incredibly quickly. And one of the things
that's been on my mind is how do we explain
to people that are not down in the weeds doing the
work, working in the clinic or working in the laboratory? How do we explain what's happening? Because I think we appreciate
that this is ultimately going to affect everyone's life, and I think that one of the ways that people learn about science, they learn about technology,
is actually through the media, through Hollywood,

powerful technologies. Who's gonna draw the limits? How are we going to move forward? And I think that's what ultimately helps. In some ways, Hollywood gets it right when its moral compass is right, when it sets our moral compass right. Hollywood gets it wrong
when it tries to tell us about science, I suppose, in a way that doesn't make any realistic sense. - So for me, I thought

- Pick one. - Pick one, okay. (audience laughs) Well, probably I should

that it was borrowed back from (mumbles). Just to move the conversation along, flipping over the question of Hollywood is a very pertinent
political question today, which is, we are living in times where the amount of distrust
for science is phenomenal and I have to tell people constantly that science is not fake news, that there's a strong line
