your life around you are dispirited and try to encourage them. It might be philanthrophically,
it might not be. It might be personally,
but it's a tough time for the graduate students
and the postdocs in my lab. I don't know if you're
feeling that as well. - So has your work and
especially maybe your writing, has that led you to have
conversations and interactions with new groups of people
that have sort of taken you in new directions? - Well, certainly politically, yes. As you know, my first book
on the the history of cancer was well read, but then Ken Burns made
a documentary out of it. The Gene is also going to be made into a documentary by Ken Burns. You should probably not tweet that until it's formally announced. (audience laughs) - [Jennifer] No tweeting. - It'll be announced in a week or two. But we're in the middle of filming. In fact, we filmed you, Jennifer. And so I think that the transition
into serious documentary, these are three hours, or
in the case of Emperor, six hour documentaries, allowed us to reach a group of people. I think one of the most
interesting conversations I've had recently is
with people who work on education for children. How do you take textbooks, which are becoming more and
more outdated in some ways, how do you convert that
into education for children? How do you make it possible to put that on the web to some extent, but supplement that with
books that are more readable? And that's an issue that
I'm very interested in. How do you make a kind of
simultaneous storytelling as well as didactic education
for AP, for instance, which is much more accessible, and doesn't remain boring or textbook? - I think kids are actually
a great way in, too. I had this great experience
a couple of years ago. My son was in seventh grade at the time and he was just taking a class. They were teaching the kids about DNA and so the teacher
asked me if I would come and talk to the kids a
little bit about my work. So I went to the school
and we did it after school, so it was just voluntary. We just asked the kids
who would like to do it and my first shock was
I walked into this room thinking there might be two, three kids. There were 20 kids there,
and 15 of them were girls. It was cool, yeah. The other thing was, so I
thought, how am I gonna get these 12 year olds
interested in what I do? So I thought, well, I'll
take this 3D printed model that I have of the Cas9 protein. It was actually made by Jacob Corn, who's here in the audience, here at the Innovative Genomics Institute. It's printed on a 3D printer, but it's based on an actual
crystallographic structure of the Cas9 protein, which is the scissors that cuts the DNA and the
RNA molecule that programs it to find and cut a
particular sequence of DNA. That's what makes it a powerful tool, and then the DNA itself getting cut. So I took this model. It's about this big. It's about the size of a football or so. I took it in. The model was designed
by Jacob very nicely so that it can be pulled apart. You can actually pull the DNA out. - [Dr. Mukherjee] You
should've brought it. - You can see where the DNA got cut. I should have brought
it, would've been fun. But I took it to the kids and I thought, well, I won't
actually take it apart. I won't show them actually
how the DNA gets cut 'cause that might just be
too complicated for the kids. So I had this model, but
it's brightly colored and it looks kinda cool. So the kids said, well,
can you pass it around? So I started passing this thing around and I was describing it to them. Within five seconds, of
course, they had pulled, they're like, oh, cool, the DNA comes out. Hey, we can pop this piece off. You know, they were dissembling it and they were looking at it
and they were figuring it out, and they were asking tons of questions. Actually, they asked a number of really interesting ethical questions. I think one of the kids
at that little meeting asked me about the ethics, not
really using that language, but asked me, what does this
mean for changing embryos, humans, and doesn't that
mean that you could decide to make somebody taller or smarter or wow? Just kind of grappling. You could see the thought process going. So I really think that the more that we can reach out to kids because kids are natural scientists. They love questions. And the more that we can do
that and get them engaged, they don't think about science as a thing. It's something that's interesting that's entered their world. - [Dr. Mukherjee] Are we
getting ready for questions? - We want to turn it over to
questions from the audience, but before we do that, I just
want to cover one more thing with you, Sid, and that
is, I would love to hear the motivation that you had
for writing your first book. Because I think, for me,
The Emperor of All Maladies, your first book about cancer,
it really is a profound work. It's very, in some ways, very depressing, but it's also incredibly
interesting to look at the history of how humans have grappled with this really intractable disease. I'd love to hear what
motivated you to do it, what was most surprising
to you about that process, and was it fun? Was it hard? Tell us about that. - So I had never thought
I would write a book. I was trained as a physician scientist, very nose to the ground, and The Emperor of All
Maladies really grew out of a patient's question, and
the patient's question was, she was in the middle of
chemotherapy for sarcoma, and she said, why are we doing
this and where are we going? And it seemed to me just astonishing that here was this disease
that has occupied our culture. Cancer has become more than a disease. It is a metaphor. It is an allegory. People use it to describe states of mind. Very few illnesses in
human history have ascended to this kind of space in human culture. And yet we had no real history, we had no book about it in the same sense. People had written about
cancer in a thousand ways. There were a million textbooks on it. So that was really the
beginning of that book. I started as a fellow and during the day, I
would be in the clinic and in the evenings, I would write. It was just a journal to start with and it grew and grew and grew, and at some point of time, I decided that I would
show it to a publisher. Someone said to me, you know
there are gonna be two readers for that book, your mother and you? (audience laughs) 600 pages on cancer. When it was first delivered, someone said it was
like a phone directory, from in the days when there
used to be phone directories, a phone directory from hell. (audience laughs) it had a black cover. In the back, in white, it
had cancer written on it, and it was 1,800 pages long. It had cut down to 600 pages. So that's how I started. That was my first book. - Fascinating, yeah. (laughs) Sounds hard, working in
the clinic during the day and writing at night. Wow, I'm amazed that you could do that. And your second book? So, well received as well. Many people have read The
Gene that I've run into just sort of randomly. But also you encountered some
criticisms about the book. Tell us about that experience. - So, again, it's the
experience of distilling very complex science to simple. You can't satisfy everyone. The simplification is
absolutely necessary. Again, it was delivered
at 1,800-odd pages. It had to be cut down to 600 and I have to do most of the cutting. So things get left on the floor. Things have to be cut off
and left on the floor. So you have to have a
radical simplification in many places and scientists
don't like being simplified. They don't like their
work being simplified, but in order to communicate
with a much wider audience, you have to take away terminology, take away even people. Too many names becomes word salad. Readers get switched off. If you use too much terminology,
people get switched off. So that's one. So there's the criticisms
of omission is one area. One of the controversial
parts the book involved questions like race and IQ. I really thought that for
this book, it was odd. This was long before these questions had become very central. So there's a chapter on
race and a chapter on IQ, both of which were read by
people I respected in the field. Actually, Marcus Feldman over at Stanford, who you know very well,
was a very important person who read the chapter on race. The chapter on IQ was also
read by many, many people in the psychiatric community. I really felt that those
were important to put in. In retrospect, I feel even more strongly that they were important to put in, but there are disagreements about some of these fundamental questions. So it's part of the territory. - [Jennifer] Yeah, absolutely. - So maybe I'll continue this, since we're speaking
about books and words. You know, I'm very sensitive
to the idea of words around, words like war on
cancer, battle on cancer. Some people, some patients don't like it. One woman famously said to
me, you go fight the war. This is not a word that I want to own. Others want to own that word. It somehow helps. The idea of them fighting something in their own bodies helps them. What do you feel about
the word gene editing? Tell us what the various words have been and what did you coin yourself? What do you say yourself
when you use these words? Genetic surgery has been
used to describe this. What's your sense of any of this? - Yeah, I think we've seen, I've noticed an interesting evolution in
language around gene editing. So it started off as people
would pretty much universally call it genome engineering, right? Genome engineering. Obviously CRISPR was not the
first way to modify genomes. There were earlier
technologies for doing this, and they really were
engineering in the sense that you actually had to
engineer proteins individually to make targeted changes to DNA in cells, and that involved a lot of work and a lot of engineering in the lab, engineering of proteins. When CRISPR came along, initially the same
language was applied to it, but what's happened, and
this wasn't me at all. This just sort of happened
organically, I think, in the field, is that people began to adopt the language gene editing. Why? Well, I think it's because
it sort of reflects the maybe simpler nature
of this technology in the sense that it doesn't
require a lot of engineering for this tool to be employed in-- - [Dr. Mukherjee] When did
you hear that phrase first, Jennifer? - Gene editing?
- Gene editing, yeah. - Gene editing. I think it started sometime
back around the time that we had the first international
conference in Washington at the end of 2015 on gene editing, and I think we used that
language for that meeting. Maybe at some level, that
started to permeate the language that was used to describe
it in other contexts. But I started to ask people,
people like George Church, when do you use editing and
when do you use engineering? And I think it was George that said, well, I really think that the
new ways of modifying genes are much more like
editing than engineering because we don't have to
engineer anything to do this. We can just use the tool. - Right, and do you think, moving forward, this term will stick? Is it easy for you now to use this? Do you find there's a facility with it? - I think so. I mean, I'm curious to know,
and I try to ask this question of people that are not scientists, ask them how they react to that. What does that mean to you
when someone says gene editing? Does that mean anything
or is it just opaque? But I think it's fairly
descriptive of what it is that we're really doing. Because if you think about
it, it's really a tool, really a technology that's
all about rewriting DNA. We've been able to synthesize DNA. So we can write it, we can erase it, we can cut and paste it,
and now we can rewrite it. So I think it really is sort of analogous to having a text editor that you're using on the genetic code. - One of the astonishing things, I don't know how many people have worked with the CRISPR-Cas9 system. One of the astonishing things
as a user in human biology is how simple it is. It is very, very simple, and maybe that's part of the captured in this idea of gene editing. It seems to me, one thought I
had is that for a long time, I struggled with it to figure
out how it would help us therapeutically in cancer. Of course, to elucidate targets in cancer, to genetically manipulate cancer cells, it's really, really simplified, but could we use this
therapeutically in cancer? And I struggled with that for awhile, and, of course, the
answer has now come to us. You can't necessarily
use it in cancer cells because evolution is working against you. You have to essentially get it, we think, in 100% of the cancer cells. Otherwise, the ones that have
not been edited will evolve and take over. Maybe you can do it over and over again, but the fact that the immune system has now become quite clearly
one of the mechanisms of controlling cancer, and immune cells, you
can manipulate and edit, and thereby reintroduce them. So it's really given us a powerful way to think about cancer, not from the standpoint of cancer itself, but from the microenvironment
around cancer. Couple of questions that arise. One is it seems to me that
editing sperm and eggs, or sperm and egg making
cells, is going to be easier than doing it in the entire embryo. What do you think are
the prospects of that and is that something, do you
see yourself, your own lab, ever moving in that direction? Making edited human sperm and eggs? - So I think you're absolutely right, and my disclaimer is that I'm not a human developmental
biologist by any stretch, but I think that from what
I've come to understand from talking to people that
are experts in this area, I think that this is absolutely coming, that it's going to be possible, and it's gonna obviate the
question of editing human embryos because you won't need to.
- Which is much harder. - Right, you won't need to. You'll edit the sperm and eggs. And would I ever do this in my own lab? No. (laughs) Not for any reason other than that's not the kind of
biologist that I am. That's not the kind of
expertise that I have. But I think that you're right that in terms of thinking
about future clinical impacts, this is an area of very active development where there's likely to be real advances. - One piece of conversation
we were having earlier, which is an important thread to pick up, is that it seems that a
lot of the conversations have focused around gene editing, but we're also seeing the
simultaneous development of other technologies. You mentioned one of them,
embryology, stem cell biology, and the third one is artificial
intelligence, deep learning. I have a couple of thoughts about deep learning and genetics, particularly from the
standpoint of cancer, but I'd love to hear
your thoughts as well. One thing that occurred to me
was deep learning is beginning to elucidate things about our genome that I did not think possible before. I read this fascinating study of early cardiovascular disease. So if you take cardiovascular disease and you ask the question, how many patients with
early cardiovascular disease can be explained by single gene mutations? And this is how we grew up as biologists. We thought about mutation in
a gene affecting a pathway, thereby causing the change
that leads to a disease. Very classical genetic model of thinking. And cardiovascular disease,
familial hypercholesterolemia, you get elevated cholesterol. You have all sorts of problems. This study looked at
if you take 100 people, only two of them, two of
those 100, can be explained by these sort of powerful
single gene mutations that will increase risk. The question is, what about the rest? What about the other 98? And for a long time, we
didn't really know how to solve that problem. My colleagues in complex
genomics tell me now that deep learning is beginning to solve these kinds of problems,
that, in fact, it turns out that complex human phenotype
can often be explained by what I would call not shove effects. So those single genes were like shoves. They would push you
strongly in one direction. But by nudge effects. Often hundreds or often even
thousands of gene variants that nudge you towards, you know, have small effect
in and of themselves, but as a network, or maybe in the context, move you even a little
bit, even slightly towards your ultimately. So the question really arises
is that if that's going to be the case with most human diseases, does gene editing help with nudge effects? Can you imagine ways or would
it only help with shove genes? Are we gonna reach some
kind of biological limit, as it were, to the capacity
to manipulate human phenotype? It's a complicated question. So I don't know what your
thoughts about that are. - Well, I have two thoughts about that. I mean, one is that I think that CRISPR and gene editing technologies
that are coming from that are going to help with nudge effects in the sense that they're
gonna help us uncover all the genes that are in those networks, and that's already happening. So there's lots and
lots of laboratories now that are using gene
editing, not in the clinic, but they're using it to understand the genetics of human disease. And they're doing it both in
human cells and organoids, which are cultured bits of organs that you can grow in the lab, as well as in animal models of disease. So I think that's gonna
be a very powerful way. And frankly, I totally agree
with what you're saying about using artificial
intelligence or machine learning to help us understand those networks 'cause they're often complicated and you have to really
understand all the players that might be contributing
to a particular trait, for example. But the other way that I
think that gene editing will potentially have an impact
clinically in the future, and we're not there today, but I think the technology
is going in that direction, is being able to edit or
modify multiple genes at once. - [Dr. Mukherjee] What's up with that? - Well, sometimes these genes are found in very disparate parts
of the genetic material, but sometimes they're
actually co-localizing in three dimensional space,
and there's more and more that's being learned about
how that works in cells. So I'm thinking that in the
future it may be possible to use gene editing to alter
multiple genes at once, maybe to remove whole segments of a genome that aren't necessary for certain kinds of developmental pathways, for example, in particular cell types. I think the opportunities
for using it as a real tool of understanding of the
genome are still really very much out there to be captured. - So let me turn the
question that you asked me in the beginning of the
conversation back to you. What does the phrase human
evolution mean to you in 2018? - What does the phrase human
evolution mean to me in 2018? - [Dr. Mukherjee] How
has that changed for you? - Well, I think we're on
this incredible continuum. It really is an exciting time. I feel many days a sense of wonder. I feel amazed that I'm alive at this time when we're at this moment
when all these technologies are coming together
and, for the first time, we can do things. Like we said, in Chancellor
Christ's introduction, we really have now the
power to control evolution, and it's not just in principle our own. That's obviously still
on the leading edge, but to control evolution
of other organisms in our environment. It's a really profound opportunity and a profound responsibility. So I hope that we can all work together and work as you're doing to educate people about the science behind this
so they can think about it and really contemplate what this means. - [Dr. Mukherjee] Is it possible to have an international moratorium until we decide this is conceivable? - No. (laughs) No, it's really not, because
as we discussed earlier, I think culturally there
are just many differences in the way people approach these things, and how would you enforce such a thing? But I think what one can
do is engage in discussions that are international, invite
people to share their views, try to understand where
they're coming from, and I think that's what
universities should be doing. We should be encouraging that and be really leading that conversation. Not dictating it, but just inviting it and welcoming different points of view. So I would like to now open
up the floor for questions, 'cause I know that some
of you may have questions, and we'd really like to

Yes? - [Man] Hi, thanks, it was a great talk. My question is sort of quick. I'm just wondering what have been the three most
influential-slash-favorite books for both of you. - Well, it's interesting that you ask because we were just

- There you are, okay. - [Woman] You talked earlier

eagerness to be engaging in the scientific process. People that have felt
maybe not included in that in the recent history
and wanna get into it, and wanna be recognized for their work. They wanna be prominent. They wanna attract attention. They want to make progress. So I think there are
motivations that go beyond the simple joy of discovery, which I think all scientists share. There also are people

microcultures in science are different. We were talking about,
you know, once in a while, I'll speak with someone
like George Church. I actually had a public
conversation with George. I talked about, George is a big enthusiast of really opening this field up. I think it's fair to say
he would be an enthusiast of making directed manipulations
in human sperm and eggs. And he thinks, that's his brain. I think like a physician first. My first thing that
comes out of my brain is, "Are we ending suffering?" "How can I first do no
harm?" is the first thing that comes out of my brain. People's brains are hooked up differently, and I do think that
people's cultural brains are hooked up differently. Yes, there's a need for provocation. Yes, there's a need to do as you work to put people on the map, but someone who's grown
up in, I grew up in India, the cultural brain, you know,
what we think is permissible and not permissible is different. We have different
understanding, for instance, of where life begins. And that is important. And it's so important that,
in fact, there will be places, there are places that are drawing lines, which then the lines don't
exist across the border. Science will move in accordance to that. If you wanna make genetic
changes in human sperm and eggs, as a scientist, you would
relocate to a place, if you badly wanted to do that, you'd find a place to do it in the world. So that's why I keep coming
back to this question. We need to have some sense of boundary. That's my physician brain speaking, first do no harm brain speaking. We have to have some boundaries. I don't know how to reach them, but I think they're important. - [Man] Hi, question up here. So one of the most surprising
things about genetics for me was that effectively 97% of
DNA itself is noncoding, right? And for awhile, there was
sort of the dead space and you just assume that introns
were there for no reason, and every time I look
at the literature still, it's always kind of vague and confusing, and it's like, well,
introns may do something, they may have some role. But I guess my question is,
what is the current attitude towards introns in general,
with respect to DNA, and do you think CRISPR
will ever become so advanced such that these kinds of concerns are going to become necessary in whether we're gonna understand
something else about that? I understand that's kind of theoretical. (audience laughs) - We're both pointing at each other. - Well, let's explain
some terminology first, just to widen out the question. So one of the surprising things that we learned as biologists, in fact, this happens to be the 40th year of the discovery of gene splicing. And there was a big
symposium, I went to it. So it turns out that, in
fact, a gene in the genome is often broken up into parts, and they're long parts that
are spliced out or removed when that gene finally
becomes a mature RNA, which then gives a protein. So introns are those long
parts that are removed. Exons are the parts that
ultimately make their way into becoming protein, and the question has to do with, well, what about all those
introns and also the other space, the intergenic spaces, which for a long time
were called junk DNA? That's a terms that we
inherited from the 19... The history's interesting of that term. We inherited that term
for really no good reason. We inherited that term
from the 1960s and 1970s when our understanding of the genome was much, much more limited. My one thought about this is for cancer, it's turning out that those introns and that the intergenic
spaces are turning out to be extraordinarily important. Not all of it, but much of it. Jennifer, you refer to some of this. It's not only important in terms of regulating gene function. It is important in terms of
the way DNA folds in space and that also regulates gene function. Some parts of it will turn
out to be unimportant. There are lots of viruses
that sank their way into the genome, which have been silenced. In fact, there are whole
systems to silence these viruses that might pop their
heads up from the genome. So it's an active space, but all I can tell you is that in cancer, these previously
nonfunctional elements of DNA are turning out to becoming
extraordinarily important. And, by the way, how are we finding out that they're extraordinarily important? By using CRISPR. By chucking them out
and all of sudden seeing that they, in fact,
influence gene function in ways that we hadn't
thought about before. In cancer, that's a huge area of research, but I don't know what your thoughts are. - [Jennifer] Yeah, I don't
have anything to add. That's exactly right. - [Man] Over here. - Yeah, sorry. - [Man] My question is
regarding the accessibility of gene therapy and CRISPR systems. I mean, currently a treatment with antisense oligonucleotides
is $400,000 a year, and do you ever see these technologies helping the entire world, or just more well-developed nations? - Yeah, well, it's a great question. It's a really important question, and something that I am
thinking about a lot. I think this is on a
lot of people's minds, and whenever there's a new technology, you ask, what is this doing
to really help people globally versus creating even
more inequalities, right? And I think we'd all like
to see it do the former and not the latter. But inevitably when you
have new technologies, it's expensive to develop those and if you want to motivate
companies especially to do the work that's necessary
to create a therapeutic and go through all the clinical
trials that are necessary, there is a big investment involved, and that does lead to high costs of those kinds of treatments. How do you deal with that? So one of the things that
I'm involved in right now is some very active,
very exciting discussions with our clinical colleagues
at UC San Francisco. So through the Innovative
Genomics Institute, we've been working on something called the Genome Surgery Center. It's in its fledgling stages, but the real idea behind
this is to address exactly the issue that you're raising. I think none of us want
to see these technologies develop to help the .01%. We really want to see them develop to help a much larger swath of people. And so how do you do that? How do you get there and
deal with the realities of the costs of development? So there's some really creative ideas that are coming out of this. We have here, of course, at UC Berkeley, we have a lot of people
that are thinking about this from the standpoint of
economics, sociology, history, philosophy that we're engaging with. Also at Stanford University. And so, we have this great
intellectual community here in the Bay Area to really tackle this, and I'd really like to see us
take that on very directly, and find ways to develop, for example, just to give you an example,
what if you could find a way to treat a large swath of people that have a particular genetic disease using one configuration of
a gene editing technology that wouldn't require
individual clinical trials for each one? This would really reduce costs. So we're looking into ways
that we can streamline that, and to do that, we really
need to bring together people that are doing the technology development with those that are clinicians. They know their patients and they know what the technology
needs to be able to do. It's a great challenge and
we're, I think, on the cusp of being able to tackle it. - I mean, sickle cell
disease is going to be one of the first examples of this. How we tackle it is really
gonna be one of the models. Because, as a hematologist, I've taken care of hundreds of patients, young men and women, often

Um so, um where does the story start? People have written about cancer since in fact the beginning of writings in medicine. This is the Edwin Smith Papyrus from 2500 BC, which was subsequently translated, but case 45 is a particularly moving case, in which there's a description which sounds very much like a description of breast cancer. Of course, the word cancer doesn't exist. It comes across comes into human vocabulary much much later, and even when it first comes into human vocabulary, the word karkinos for crab is really a kind of lumpy word. It lumps together tumors of various sorts, including some benign, malignant. It really refers to a mass or a lump looking like a crab buried under the sand. That said, the disease cancer, like many diseases, existed um centuries, millennia before our understanding of the word cancer, even the word cancer itself. I find it an uncanny thought from the standpoint of medical humanities. Um So, I talked about the word cancer, and and and the physiology of cancer, the idea of what cancer was physiologically. People spent a lot of time thinking about that physiology, of the pathophysiology of cancer. This is a supposed bust of Galen. And Galen was among the first to think about a systematic way of describing why cancer happened. Galen believed in, as many of you know, the humoral theory, that the body was was considered was composed of four cardinal humors, black bile, yellow bile, phlegm, and blood. And it was a kind of And every disease could be explained by some kind of aberration of these four fluids, a kind of hydraulic pathology. One thing went up, the other thing went down. Pneumonia was phlegm going up and red blood going down, or some version of that. And of course, for cancer, he reserved black bile. Um black bile is this fluid that had really never really been seen, but was somehow reserved for cancer for one other disease, and that was depression. In fact, melancholy literally means black bile. And so, this idea that this this the the twin aspects of of a physiological or a physical aberration and a psychic aberration, cancer and depression somehow entwined together. Um And of course, one thing that that that we know from from the past is that every time there's a there's a there's a there's a claim of of of understanding of cancer, that claim leads to an attempt to that metaphor of that claim, the the idea behind it, what it means, leads to an attempt to cure cancer based on that idea. Um so, the idea of of black bile immediately suggested that the that the way to cure cancer would be to remove black bile from the body. Here's a a Sculptetus mastectomy, which is brutal in and of itself. But what's interesting about it is that it was followed by a kind of purging ritual, in which they would try to attempt to move the black bile out of the local site of the body. Um that is itself interesting, but what's also very moving to me is this image from the late 1500s of of post-surgical care. Here's a Here's a woman that after her surgery has been bound up with an attempt to to produce some kind of a brace or or a palliation to allow her to live her life after the mastectomy. Um The modern understanding of cancer doesn't come about until much later. This is a a process this this this kind of knowledge in cancer it repeats itself over and over again. But the idea that cancer was a disease of malignant cells um was something that was brought by brought into being by several people but but remarkably by uh by Rudolf Virchow. Um Virchow looked down the microscope and began to realize that despite cancers arising from a variety tissues that there was a commonality between all of them lung brain cancer uh cancer of the liver and the stomach in all cases that there were cells that had were dividing abnormally. Um and so all of a sudden these diffuse entities uh as I said that the the lumping of the term cancer began to make some kind of physiological sense as a disease of cells. Cells were dividing abnormally dividing where they should not be dividing. It was a disease of of cell division and Virchow coined the term transformation uh and it was used subsequently after Virchow as well to describe the process by which a normal cell would become a cancer cell. Now that it's an interesting word because it implies two things. One is that Virchow based on his work had already figured out that cancer cells had to arise from normal cells. Now if cancer cells arose from normal cells then it so factor it follows that something must have happened to a normal cell just all of a sudden or maybe not all of a sudden make it into an an abnormal cell a cell that was dividing abnormally invading other tissues etc. So already in Virchow the question had been set up what was that something? What was that something that had happened to a perfectly normal cell that all of a sudden made it impossible for it to obey normal laws of cellular division and begin to divide normally. And if you read Virchow you you begin to understand that these questions had an elemental quality about them. Virchow wondered why wounds heal why if you make a cut in your in in your in a in your hand that wound heals and it closes itself but you don't grow a new hand. Um but in but but but but when a cancer cell divides it it reaches a place and then continues to divide Virchow thought it thought and then begins to invade other places. So so there was a there was a really a ruminative process of trying to tease out what cancer was in terms of cells. One idea that came out of that was that if cancer was a lump of cells was a mass of growing cells growing abnormally then we should be able to cut it out and remove those cells and thereby cure cancer. Um so this led to um the the golden age of cancer surgery. Um here is um uh uh uh Halsted William Stewart Halsted um who really would take that idea to um to its its its a kind of um manic perfection. Uh the idea that if if you removed cancer cells from the body uh that you would ultimately be able to effect the kind of cures uh that would that had been so fleetingly impossible in in years past. And for Halsted really that meant um Halsted is a fascinating character. I've I've written about him there have been sort of several important books and apparently now a new television documentary or or serial of some sort on Halsted. Uh I haven't watched it. So um but Halsted is a fascinating character because um Halsted was um among other things uh he he went to Europe where he witnessed the birth of surgery. Um he was a brave experimentalist and he began to use a chemical that they had that they had been using in in um in uh in Europe uh which was a derivative of cocaine to try to produce anesthesia. And in fact began to inject himself as an experimental subject and very soon he was addicted to cocaine. Um and so then he was sent he came from a wealthy family from New York and he was sent to a sanatorium to detoxify himself and the chosen drug for uh the detoxification of a cocaine addiction in those days was another medicine called morphine. So um so then he became uh doubly addicted to cocaine and morphine. Um and and the legend goes that he would scrub out of surgery inject himself privately in his in his rooms. But Halsted's um powerful idea again based on the cellular theory of cancer the passes powerful idea was that if you extirpated the cells if you threw those if you cut the cells out of the body and and remove the mass then you'd actually begin to cure cancer. Um and then he made a bold an even bolder hypothesis and that was that if that was the case then removing not just the not just the cells themselves but the tissue around it um would be would would actually get more cures. Um and so he on one hand was technically perfecting sur- surgical practice but on the other hand also began beginning to advance this uh radical hypothesis um that in fact if you cut more you would cure more. And he thought he would use breast cancer as his as his prime example and he began to edge along with his many students began to edge towards the radical the ultra radical and the super radical mastectomy cutting away more and more tissue in the hopes of curing um curing women of breast cancer. So the question as we edge towards the 1980s in the field was if those efforts had really come to a kind of plateau should we go back to the drawing board and start to reimagine what cancer was. What is cancer? Um what is cancer? Where does it begin? And and what is the commonality? Why is it that cells in these diffuse parts of the body um have have started undergoing this this process of malignant division? And that brought us right back to Virchow uh to this idea of transformation. What had happened to that normal cell to convert it into an abnormal cell? And there were four major ideas about what had happened. Uh one was heredity. So this is a Brazilian ophthalmologist who had observed cancer moving through multiple generations in the eye. Um so so he and others had thought that some hereditary component uh later called genes had to be involved. Um this is Percivall Pott who thought the environment had to have a role because um he had a Pott had observed um that chimney sweeps going up and down chimneys young boys had a high incidence of scrotal cancer and something in the in the chimney soot must be causing this this cancer. Um Uh for the longest time people knew that chance was involved um because you could get cancer sporadically within a family with a heavy history of cancer you could get cancer sporadic sporadically even within that. And just uh down the road um Peyton Rous had um argued that viruses were the cause of cancer. So it was a little bit like a like the the blind humans and the elephant um in which everyone had a theory but they were feeling parts of the theory. Somehow or the other these four pieces had to be resolved. And that was ultimately resolved in the 1980s um by by bench scientists by basic scientists who made made the observation now central to to the theory of cancer um that alterations in growth controlling genes cause cancer. So all of a sudden these four pieces began to make sense. You could alter the growth controlling genes by right you can inherit the the genes, the altered genes. BRCA1 mutation is one example, Lynch syndrome another example. Those genes could be mutated by the environment, carcinogens. In fact, interestingly, Pott coined the term carcinogen way back when, long before it was even known what it could be. They could be mutated by chance and and viruses could either mutate them or or create circumstances where the genes would become mutated in cells by carrying in genetic material or causing inflammatory stimuli and so forth. So, all of a sudden these four theories began to converge on the idea that genes were centrally involved. And this cancer all of a sudden made that transition, that leap from a a disease of cells to a disease of genes. And what's important about that was it was also realized that these very genes, genes that control growth and metabolism and are mutated in cancers are have normal functions. So, in fact, cancer was a distortion of the normal of normalcy. Uh the very genes that cause our embryos to grow uh turned out to be the genes that cause that when mutated cause cancers to grow abnormally. The very genes that allow our wounds to heal or cells to move um are abnormally regulated or abnormally mutated or mutated, sorry, or abnormally regulated in cancer. So, and and I I I like this phrase that was picked up from from an old uh the the the tale from Beowulf that cancer was a distorted version of our normal cells. So, the question then turned to if alterations in growth controlling genes cause cancer, how many are there and how common are they? Is it one gene? What is the So, going back to Warburg's house question, is it one gene per cancer? Is it three genes per cancer? Um The the the the writer Lewis Thomas who I'm a an enormous fan of, a physician writer, um at practice, you know, was at NYU for years um wrote about an astonishing moment in his lifetime when he began to see the application of the rational precepts of medicine on the therapy of of people. And that's sort of where we're edging towards oncology of I just it's it's I've likened it to being the adolescence of a field where we're in kind of peculiar adolescence where we're starting to understand or try to understand physiology and launch this this grand experiment. And that means that when we've looked to find gene, you know, the idea that we would find the Gleevec for every cancer has turned out to be not easy and in fact not successful. We've tried to look for these Gleevec-like sort of exquisite molecules that turn off and cancer-causing gene products but we haven't found them very easily. But rather than doing that, the question is if you imagine that these genes don't act in in or genetic networks don't act by themselves, they act in networks, they act together much like normal cells. Much like the physiology of normal cells, could you interrupt something specific about cancer's physiology? For instance, could you somehow interrupt the physiology of growth, its metabolism, its capacity to escape from the immune system, etc.? And for a for a wider public, you could you could make this analogy of of a of a machine of the cellular cellular machine rather than attack its individual components which we were trying so desperately to do when we saw when I saw that transformation with with drugs like Gleevec, could there be pieces of physiology? Could there be not just the cogs but whole little systems of physiology that were being attacked in this case the the so-called hallmarks of cancer. And that's we're trying to do that now by stitching together various therapies, immunological therapies and various other therapies. So to to to to return to the hypothesis which I think is is is the is the intriguing hypothesis, the question that we're asking ourselves is will this triangle help us? Will early detection, genetic annotation, and biological annotation, and personalized therapy somehow or the other yield the cures that we haven't been able to achieve in the past? So so just to summarize this again, the question that that that that I'm intrigued by is is what have we learned in these last 10 odd years? It seems that we've learned that targeting a cancer's unique vulnerabilities particularly in combination yields successes for some cancers. We've also learned that targeting a cancer's microenvironment and its physiology, so not just its cellular physiology but the physiology around it is possible and effective in some cancers. And that early therapy including definitive local therapy, surgical therapy, is more effective than late therapy for some cancers. And again, I just remind myself of ourselves of that pivotal Herceptin trial in which we took patients who were who had who had metastatic disease versus early disease. And and this again leads me to wonder about this grand experiment. Early detection plus deep personalization including genetic annotation of tumor environment plus targeted therapy in combination against tumor environment, will this get to get get us to that place? You might have noticed I haven't talked about prevention at all. The reason I haven't talked about prevention is that it's been quite difficult to get prevention into this funnel as it were. It's because for some reason or the other our capacity to eliminate major carcinogens including smoking have suffered, although we've identified those carcinogens have suffered because of the appropriate deployment. But on the other hand, for the last 10 or 15 odd years, we haven't really found major preventable chemical carcinogens. We've looked, maybe we haven't looked deep enough, maybe we haven't looked with the right methods but but major preventable chemical carcinogens with large public health impact have been hard to find in the West. There are still some in the in the world at large, viruses certainly qualify. We found some important ones. But in in in in this population, in this audience, when people ask me, "What should I do? What what what chemical carcinogen can I remove from my own living my lifestyle, my environment?" It's been hard for me aside from smoking. It's been hard for me to to to to to come up with with reasonable answers as to what they are. And that's going to be a major challenge as we move forward. But but from the from the standpoint of treatment, this this is this is an experiment that we that we are certainly launching right now. And and here's a here's the here's sort of the the full experiment which is, you know, could we combine early detection, deep personalization, and targeted therapy and then do maintenance and surveillance, basically bring patients back into the same circle so that they would once their cancer was in remission or or maybe was been removed has comes back. Uh in terms of storytelling, I I maybe I'll finish with this this one story. This is my friend um uh who is a young man. This is a picture of him taken eight years ago. My friend Will Kirsky who um has has a a tremendously aggressive malignant melanoma. Um and he received um long cycle of immunotherapy. Um was in a very was in a deep remission for about a year and a half and then sadly recurred and relapsed and is now on a second second trial of a second targeted therapy. He's basically going through this through this through this process. Um one thing that struck me, I don't know I've written about this, I don't know how many people had a chance to read it. One thing that struck me is that when I saw this picture taken eight years ago, this is Will at a at a friend's wedding. Um you think you can see that culprit lesion right there the the melanoma right there. Um but in fact, you'd be wrong. The real culprit lesion is this one. Uh just underneath his hairline. That other lesion is still remains and is completely has not progressed. It looks to you know, looks like a malignant lesion to every dermatologist and yet that one has not progressed whereas the one under his ear which which was barely visible became the aggressive malignant melanoma. And that is one of the things that of course is part of detecting these cancers early. We are using beginning to use deep learning tools including this one. This was published recently which which take things that are to the human eye look just as malignant as any others but then reclassify them using algorithms we don't even understand. We don't understand because these tools are black boxes and reclassifies them as malignant versus benign hoping to pick up this this melanoma. Um and one of the major concerns or talks is how on earth are we going to then treat? I mean if this melanoma is picked up in in in a in a human being and removed, do you then give them that same kind of therapy with Herceptin, some kind of drug like Herceptin which will extend their lives and at what cost? What what is the what is the what is this what is the cycle then this patient enters into and how on earth are we going to afford these kinds of cyclical therapies in which patients cycle between early detection, personalization, targeted therapy and then return back to maintenance and surveillance? So um I wanted to end therefore by talking taking taking a step back. Um I've tried not only to capture something about cancer as a as a medical experiment as a biological experiment but also as a cultural experiment. Cancer has of course invaded our lives, our vocabularies in a way that was would have been unprecedented in the in in the late 1800s. Um Um the the the sociologist Erving Goffman writes about the idea of a total institution. A total institution is a place of work where a great number of similarly situated people together lead an enclosed formally administered round of life. Total institutions have their own vocabularies. They have rituals. They have cultures. They have They have rights of leaving and entering. There is the the guilt of survivorship in an institution when others have departed it. There's a guilt of departure from the institution. There's a language that surrounds you in that institution itself. Um I wonder whether as we enter this grand experiment of oncology in cancer therapy in the mid-2010s where the surveillance and the possibility of overdiagnosis and overtreatment and the idea of previvorship, the idea that you're a survivor of a disease that you haven't yet had. A woman with BRCA1 mutation is a survivor of breast cancer even though she hasn't yet had breast cancer. A man with a pre-melanotic mole treated as a pre-melanotic mole is a survivor of melanoma that he or she has not yet had but is receiving preventive treatment for that future melanoma. Um So I wonder whether we we we run the risk in this language of creating a total culture and the total institution and what that means for the lives of patients. Um so on that note just spanning, you know, epic narratives become individual narratives when you change the focus to individual humans. of their capacity to resist drugs that are targeted to the mutations that drive their growth hmm and I would say that we as a community of oncologists have turned our heads away from the idea in the 2000s and 1990s that oh you know there was a linear idea you know find the mutations Target the mutations kill the cancer cells end of story right it has not worked out to be that so we need a new paradigm and the new paradigm is to say that you know that old idea was a good idea but it wasn't right and so we need a new paradigm a new paradigm has to be that we need to Target fundamental physiological mechanisms in a cancer cell s that are different from a normal cell and thereby Target cancer it's a completely new idea and I cannot emphasize how revolutionary and how much of a difference this is in the standard thinking about cancer the standard thinking about cancer is oh you have cancer sequence it find the mutations and find the drugs that will Target the mutations right that has not worked it has not worked not only with minor mutations it has not worked with mutations that are known to be the major drivers of the cancer cell so we can put like ostriches our heads in the sand and say oh you know let's go on this way that has not worked and if we keep putting our heads like ostriches and sand it will keep not working we need to find a new way forward which lies outside this kind of linear logic of find the cancer find the mutation and find a drug that blocks the mutation that is not working one thing I would add I think what Sid said is so important because it totally undercuts the prevailing Orthodoxy and I think it takes a lot of courage and I'm not sure there are a lot of people with enough courage to say what Sid just said that essentially ever more fine-grained discovery of smaller and smaller mutations and better chemistry is not going to solve this problem of cancer we're not gonna chemically engineer our way out of this problem I think we've got to think orthogonally and really do the physiological work to starve these tumors and let me add more to that I've tried to understand the prevailing orthodoxies and I've tried to reconcile myself with them and no one was more excited than me when we found a drug that would Target one of the great mutations drivers in cancer like eras and yet no one was more disappointed in the results and so we have got to change we have got to think of new paradigms do you feel like it's an issue of sort of ignoring something that's systemic that I mean it sounds from what you're describing like you've got a building and people are looking for the brick that's messed up but in fact the problem is like the air conditioning system like it's something that is not confined to just one little place it's more part of the how the entire system works um I've I've been saying this for forever I mean I think that cancer is a physiological disease the cancer cells are physiologically different from normal cells and the more we explore that physiology outside finding mutations is the best way to move forward um I wrote a piece in the New York Times called cancer should not be about mutation hunting cancer is a physiological disease most diseases every disease that we know of is a physiological disease but physiology I mean it integrates multiple aspects of human biology there are signals that go between cells these signals can counteract or even inactivate drugs that we send with great Precision to cancer cells and cancer is one of them so I stand by my belief that unless we understand cancer at a physiological level we're not going to be we're not going to move forward on and um let me ask you a little bit about food one of the things I've found over the years is I've spoken to different that's uh really about pills um and and other kinds of medications to accept food as part of like health and medication I think that's a great question I think the first thing is proving efficacy if asparagine in another way whatever it might be could make a difference in pediatric leukemia they're just chemicals and the idea that food is not a chemical is absurdity because of course it is so my can't people live forever? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: Well 
so organisms that you know. So it's interesting. Trees are very interesting with
this in this regard. Trees don't die of aging. They usually die of diseases. They don't go through the same
aging process that we do. At least that's that's what we
think. Know they they succumb to a
disease. If you think about. And it's going to take a little
bit longer to answer this question. If you think about
people who live for a very long time, so so-called super
centenarians, they don't die of cancer or heart attacks. They die of the failure of
regeneration. They die because their bones
can't regenerate, their cartilage doesn't. So they die
of bone bones breaking. They die of stem cell failure. Their blood system doesn't
regenerate. They die of immune collapse. So what's really the issue in
terms of these the frontier of aging is there are really two
frontiers. One of them is the conquest of
disease, cancer, heart disease and so forth, neurodegenerative
diseases, etcetera. But then there's the second
door or the second frontier, which is how do we keep
ourselves regeneratively alive? How do we keep our neurons

spent she set up a home in a one room tenament the children inhabited the room and she slept in the makeshift kitchen there's no money for food so there would be money for books at night each of the boys got half a piece of white paper and a shared pencil to finish homework I might note in passing that over the last 5 years I've written three papers that have formed the core for the discovery of three novel Cancer drugs in each case the essential idea was scribbled with a pencil on half a sheet of white paper years later when my grandmother came to live with us in Delhi the suit steel suitcase had still that had been fed across so many cities came with her but it remained in the back of her closet seals shut no one knew what it contained she would Parry questions about it and it would slowly be forgotten when she died I opened the suitcase there were of course predictable object of a refugees last grabbing MOS of ardor and Desperation some jewelry a portrait letters an old shawl pockmarked by Ms but in the midst of this wrapped in cloth were four school books books some years before she had died I'd asked her what I was to do with the contents of the suitcase and she had said mysteriously give them back but the content was so outdated there was no giving back it left me totally mystified who cares about a math textbook from the 1940s in 1986 when I was 16 I walked into the library at Berkeley University there were shelves upon shelves of books Einstein Newton the lives of a cell Darwin Russo France fenon Baldwin Dickinson Monroe in looking longingly at those overstuffed shelves from which knowledge might perhaps just pour out like golden liquid if I just gave the Shelf a small jolt I decided that I too would make a transition I would also pick up a suitcase and migrate seeking a new life new knowledge and new books it would be unseemly and frankly desecrating to make any comparison between my grandmother's migration and mine but in keeping with the family tradition I too underwent a transition and a rebirth I came to study abroad but it was only after I finished medical school in my late 20s that I returned to my my grandmother's request give them back stupid stupid me it wasn't the books with their outdated equations in Old School writing it was the responsibility that comes with Transitions and rebirths and it was perhaps fittingly a reformulation of the four offerings and perhaps you can think through them with me again I want to understand how to return love I want to understand how I can have my love returned I want to forgive I want to understand how to accept forgiveness and so there you have it a reformulation of my personal truths into personal responsibilities into action it is the ultimate form of rebirth since it enables the emergence the composting of the new you and that is the answer the purpose of finding a person truth is to transform it into an action once you finished your experiment that is once you finished the quest it is time for action but not before you will transform your truth into a responsibility and that in turn will enable the birth of your new self it does not Escape me that I titled my speech the four offerings that word offering it is operative here for it implies not just an acceptance at death but a giving at Birth do both and do them with the utmost conviction thank you so much [Music]

the genetic cause was the first clue in the hunt for the genetic cause of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy came from an unexpected place next to the sweeping lush green parklands in southeast quebec a few miles from the american border and about 20 miles south of the nearest large town sherbrooke is the town of kotikuk the name kotikuk is of abunaki origin meaning river of the land of pine trees in french the area is known as pearl de canton de last because of its natural beauty but kotikuk became known for something less beguiling the kodiku curse that took many of its citizens early with strokes and sudden death in the fall of 1957 two brothers aged 39 and 41 originally from this town were admitted to the royal victoria hospital in montreal with strokes both were found to have enlarged hearts both also came under the care of peter parray a six foot four lung specialist known for his gentle humorous teaching style fascinated by the coincidence of strokes and enlarged hearts and the two brothers hurray became convinced there must be a genetic explanation so much so that he dedicated the next few years of his life to understanding the cause of the disease in the family the brothers he learned were not alone in total he studied 27 family members asking many to come to the hospital for clinical examinations some of them several times in july 1961 he published his seminal paper hereditary cardiovascular dysplasia a form of familial cardiomyopathy in which he described 20 living affected black family members as well as another 10 who had previously died and even traced the origin of the disease back to one particular immigrant who came to quebec in 1650. in fact in the four years it took him to complete the work five family members died suddenly if ever there were a family waiting for the right genetic technology to narrow down the cause of condition it was this one genome scale sequencing of course was a long way off the human genome project wouldn't get started for decades but the linkage mapping technology was invented in 1980 and it could help with this technology and a family like the one from the pieces were in place and the seminal work describing the genetic underpinnings of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy was led by husband and wife team christine and jonathan seidman of harvard medical school together with bill mckenna in london they approached the family first characterized by dr parry and invited them to weekend reunions where they could learn about the research study and comment and consent to join more than 100 family members signed up for is different it's a little bit like saying you know if you have a master score in an orchestra you know one person could play that or that Master score very fast and get one kind of music out of it it up for Dhruv Khullar and Siddhartha Mukherjee. [ Applause ] [ Applause ] >> Dhruv Khullar: All right. Well, I was in medical
school when The Emperor of All Maladies came out. And I don't think it's
an exaggeration to say that the book changed my life. It is a book that is staggering
in its scope and ambition. And, as a medical student, I
thought, "Wow, it's possible for a book to be like that. It's possible for a
career to be like that." That you could be a
doctor who sees patients. And a writer who communicates
to the public with such nuance and narrative and beauty. So I want to just start by
saying this is a real treat. Thank you for coming
to do this with me. Maybe we can start at the
beginning of the new version. And I'll have you read a
little bit of the opening to the 15th anniversary edition. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: Great. Well, thank you for
having me, Dhruv. And we can begin at the
beginning as it were. It begins with what is
now called Part Seven. Now the amazing thing
about it is that, in adding so many more chapters,
maybe paper has become thinner. [ Laughter ] Because I had imagined that by
this time that having added four or five major new chapters. That this book would
become like, you know, something that you could kill
someone with, but it isn't. So I think the Chinese
are doing something right. [ Laughter ] I'm not supposed to say that. Anyway, it's called The
Emperor's New Journey, 2010 to 2025 and Beyond. And it begins with a poem, which
I think is an important poem. Which, sort of, in some
ways, colors the whole book. This is, of course,
from Emily Dickinson. I measure every Grief I meet
With narrow, probing, eyes. I wonder if It weighs like Mine. Or has an Easier size. I wonder if They bore it long. Or did it just begin. I could not tell
the Date of Mine. It feels so old a pain. And so this is the
introduction to the new edition. And I suppose it
answers the question, which is the first question. Which is, "Why write a book
again once it's been written?" And again, to quote, "I
wonder if They bore it long. Or did it just begin." So this is called The
Emperor's New Journey. That's the seventh part and therefore the
introduction to the new book. In the summer of 2008,
I walked into the office of a New York editor with
a large brown paper bag. That's Nan Graham. Say hello, Nan [laughs]. In it were 600 pages of my book,
The Emperor of All Maladies. My mother had looked at the brick thick black-bound
pages as I left my house. And called it the
telephone directory of hell. I sat in the editor's
waiting room, high above Sixth
Avenue, looking outside. It was one of those
magical New York afternoons when the balminess of the
air becomes intoxicating. And crowds had gathered
on the streets. But I was a trainee
in oncology in Boston and all I could see
was a landscape of future grief and anxiety. The woman laughing breezily by the bakery might be
diagnosed with cancer. Ovarian? Breast? In a few years. I imagined a man smoking a
cigarette with evident pleasure by the fountain in
a hospital gown as he went for a lung CT scan. The lenses with which I saw the
world had forever been changed. In the 15 years since the
publication of The Emperor of All Maladies, the
collective lenses through which we see cancer
have also forever been changed. New horizons have emerged in
the early detection of cancer. While innovations in treatment
built on extraordinary work in immunology and cell therapy. Have widened the tantalizing
world of personalized medicine. Some cancers considered
untreatable only a decade ago have been eradicated
in individual patients. And yet, the disease continues
to surprise and frustrate us. There is, of course,
no one cancer and there will be no one cure. And with each apparent step
forward, the vast tangle of cancer's complexity comes
more vividly into view. The complete eradication
of cancer through prevention
remains a fantasy. We hunt for new classes
of carcinogens. Yet the definition of
carcinogens does not on its own explain why
a tumor will develop in one person and not another. Technologies purporting
the early detection of cancer sprout daily. But are yet to demonstrate
that patients diagnosed with these technologies
will have improved outcomes. And even with new therapies,
many patients with cancer remain and become resistant
to treatment. Meanwhile, the cost of
cancer treatment skyrockets, presenting an insurmountable
barrier to the equitable
distribution of medical care. Through all this, cancer
remains a meme for everything that is malignant in
our society and culture. When I began writing The
Emperor of All Maladies, I had in mind a kind of
journal that would allow me to make sense of
the world of cancer. The world of cancer
that I had experienced as a young physician
in medical oncology. As I wrote, the book
became something far greater than I first imagined. It became a biography that aimed to illuminate humanity's
engagement with this terrifying and beguiling ailment. I would tell the story through
the intersection of lives and ideas, scientific theory and medical practice,
culture and belief. Traced to the sweep of history,
as well as within the intimacy of a single patient's
diagnosis and treatment. And so, in a sense, the chapters that follow constitute
an entirely new emperor. In a decade and a half, there
has been so much more learning that I realized that only a
new book could capture the horizons ahead. The new chapters are organized
simply along the three pillars of our strategy against cancer. Prevention, screening
and treatment. Capped with a chapter on a
new vision for the future. New vistas have opened
yet more new vistas for exploration,
science and medicine. And for the evolving
cultural metaphors that cancer still evokes. Prevention, cancer,
treatment and the future. I welcome you to a new book. [ Applause ] >> Dhruv Khullar:
Thank you for that. So I want to start by
rewinding back even further than the start of the new book. Which is how did the
idea first come to you? You know, when did
it first come to you? Why did it come to you? Why frame this book
as a biography? You know, not as a
scientist, not a biography of a place, but of a disease. Take us back to, I
don't know, 2005, 2008, whenever you started
thinking about this book. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Well, the new book began because no such book existed. And it was really my
own attempt to try to understand the disease. The book begins with
a very simple anecdote of a patient who comes to me. And says, "Why am I
being treated this way, in this manner, through
this protocol?" And I began to realize that this
is not a question of one person. This is a very universal
question, a question that we're
all trying to understand. How did we get here? How did cancer become
what it is today? And what will happen
in the future? And the idea of writing
the book began that way. And then -- I title
my books very late in the process of titling books. When I looked back and started
reading what was then a journal, I realized that there was a
biographical quality to it. Now, people often
say, "Well, you know, isn't this an anthropomorphic
variant of." But literally biographies
have existed of characters that are much more fictional than the role cancer
occupies in our lives. And therefore, this
is how the book began. The book began because
I was interested in very fundamental questions. Where are we going? How do we get here? What happens next? But I was also interested
in the cultural metaphors. You know, Sontag's Illness as Metaphor was a very
influential book for me and plays a very large
role in the first book. And, you know, the
writing of Oliver Sacks, the writing of Atul Gawande. And so many others had played
a very influential role in my life. And yet, I felt as if I was
trying to write something that was simultaneously
more intimate, but had the sweep
of history in it. I was not trying to write an
Oliver Sacksian case history. I was not trying to write
-- there were so many nots. I was not trying to write
a Sontagian cri de coeur about what illness meant. I was trying to write none
of them, but all of them. And I was trying to
focus on one disease. The one that, in some ways,
defines contemporary life. And so that's how
the book came about. >> Dhruv Khullar:
Were you surprised by the tremendous reception
that the book received? I mean, a lot of

Pulitzer and they said, "Oh cool, what's that?" And so, how did your life change
after the book received the type of reception that it did? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Well, my life changed forever. I mean, if you're fortunate to
get critical acclaim of this. And the book became a kind
of, again, like cancer, a kind of meme that
I hadn't expected. You know, routinely, I see
variations of words and phrases from the book appropriately
acknowledged, lifted. And so my life changed forever. In some ways, there
were things about it that I didn't want to become. I didn't want to become sort of
the cancer expert of the world. I wanted to retain the curiosity
and the intimacy that it means to be a physician and a patient. >> Dhruv Khullar: But did

Well, obviously, I look like a different person. I am a different person. My public life is different. You know, the way I intersect
the world is different. So, yes, the book
swerved my life. In a way, kind of, a river
in spate swerves your life. And the book swerved
my life, of course, in some very positive ways. But also, as I said, some
ways that were unexpected and not even desirable. Just to give you one
example, ultimately, I had to hide my email
because my inbox. Even today -- >> Dhruv Khullar:
I'm sorry about that. There was, like, 25

heart because you cannot. For so many reasons, you cannot. But they break your heart
in so many different ways because this was not the
person I wanted to become. I didn't want to

of, like a river in full spate, just deviated my life. Out of what would have
