compassionate doctor. Who is able to answer
his emails every day. >> Dhruv Khullar: Yeah. So part of the answer to
this question is contained in the book, so you'll
have to buy it if you want the full answer. But let me just preview
some of it. So in the last 15 years since
the first edition came out, what has surprised you most
about the changes in oncology or health care more generally? What are the things that
you would not have predicted in 2010, 2011, and
what are the things that you maybe did predict? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: Well, I think the most
illuminating chapters -- well, all the three
chapters contain dramatic new information. But I'll just give
you sort of headliners from all the four chapters. In cancer prevention,
the question patients or patients' families, loved
ones, are asking themselves. Is, "Why did I get cancer?" And that question is a question
that people ask themselves who have not exposed
themselves to what we know as classical cancer-causing
agents, carcinogens. They're not smokers. They've, you know, drunk
their goji berry juice for a full decade and
gone to every yoga retreat that they could have gone to. They've been good
cancer do-bees. And yet, here they are
in a blue hospital gown, getting a CAT scan
for lung cancer. And so the question, which
was an embarrassing question for the community, for our
community of physicians. Was, "What are we missing?" Are we missing something that is
in the universe of carcinogens? Is there dark matter in carcinogenesis
that we're missing? And this became a progressively
embarrassing question. What I don't write about in
the book, but which is real. Is that if you really
think about it, we had not found a
preventable chemical carcinogen of great impact on human
communities since the 1970s. And that was cigarette smoke. You know, you could say obesity
is maybe one big example. But, in some ways, you could
say this is a bizarre situation. You know, you're spending
hundreds of millions of dollars, and certainly we found
many new carcinogens. But none which had the kind
of public health impact. So the headline of
the first chapter is, we are actually now
finding new ones. But they're a form of carcinogen that we had never
expected to find. They are truly the dark
matter of carcinogenesis. I'll leave you with
that thought. The second one, so the
second area we talked about, the second chapter is on
early detection and screening. And even today, there was
an article in the Times about early detection
and screening. And how cancer rates
are going up, and some say they're going down. And the answer is that
there is a fundamental, inbuilt, mathematical reason. That early detection and screening has not worked the
way we had intended it to work. And to find out, you'll have
to read the second chapter. The third has to
do with treatment. The big missing piece there was
the immune system and the role of immunology and
inflammation in cancer. And that leads on to the
fourth, which is a new direction in which we can integrate
all of this to find a new pathway forward. >> Dhruv Khullar: So let
me push you a little bit. When I was reading the end
of the book, I was delighted to see my name in the book and
that was a real thrill for me. And then I realized it was
mostly just about my prostate and that was less exciting. So the story is that I
had written an article about getting a full body
MRI, which are now being kind of aggressively marketed. And the MRI picked up a
small lesion in my prostate. Those are graded on a
scale from one to five. It was a three, of course, so
they have no idea what it was. And it led to additional testing
and follow-up MRIs and so on. So I guess reading the
chapter on screening, you could come away
with the impression that very little works
in cancer screening. Even the traditional things
that we think might work, like colonoscopies
and mammograms. And now there are
these new modalities, like full body MRIs
or liquid biopsies. So maybe just spend
a moment talking about the difficulty
of cancer screening. Why it is the case that even
today medicine has so little to offer people who
want to be proactive about getting screened. And what you make of
these newer modalities that are now on the horizon. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Yeah, so the problem with cancer screening is
a mathematical problem. It is not a cancer problem, it
is not a biological problem, it's a mathematical problem. And it's a simple
mathematical problem. I describe it in the book. There are many mathematical
problems, but there's one fundamental one. And the one fundamental
one is -- this is a thought exercise
that I'll throw out to you. And this is in the book, so you'll read about
it in the book. But imagine there are twins, in the book I call
them Hope and Prudence. Who get diagnosed with --
or who don't get diagnosed. Who get breast cancer at
the same moment of time, at the same second
as the clock ticks. One of them, Hope,
decides that she is going to be very proactive
about her health. Goes through her scans,
and a mammogram finds that she has breast cancer. And, you know, surgeons
operate on it, they give her chemotherapy,
et cetera. And she dies five years later after her cancer
has been detected. Prudence says, you know,
"I'm not doing any of this. I'm going to go to Mexico,
have wheatgrass juice. And I'm not participating in all of this nonsense,
scanning, et cetera." And she is not diagnosed until her breast cancer
becomes either visible or she starts having symptoms. And that's much later. And she gets diagnosed
at that point of time. And then Hope and Prudence are
put into the same hospital bed, and they unfortunately
die in the same moment of time as the clock ticks. So now ask yourself
the question, the word survival
comes up, right? So how many years
did Hope survive? And the answer is, well,
she survived five years. She had breast cancer,
and then she was detected, treated, et cetera. She survives five years. How many years did
Prudence survive? She survives one year,
because she gets detected at one year, and then she dies. But of course, that
couldn't be true. Because both of them
acquired their breast cancer at the same moment of time. And they died at the
same moment of time. So Hope's screening did
nothing to her, really. Both of them had the exact
same moment of death. So the only way that you can
really judge a cancer screening test is by using
mortality, not survival. Survival is easy to judge, because you can put
people on a trial. And you can have
two control groups and have one group saying, you
know, they went through the test and they survived five years. The other one didn't go through
the test and survived one year, so therefore the test works. >> Dhruv Khullar: Yeah. So I guess that makes
sense to me. But what I don't understand --
so maybe the mental model a lot of people have is incorrect. And you can correct
it if that's the case. But there's a few cells, and
they have some mutations. And they develop
into a small tumor. And it becomes a
stage one cancer. And over time it gets bigger. And it spreads to lymph nodes. And it spreads distantly. And we call that stage
two, three, four, right? And the idea is that if you were
to screen, you could catch it at stage one or stage two. And you could intervene such that Hope would
actually have lived longer than Prudence in this example. And why is that so hard? Why can't we catch it? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
And that's the problem. I'll offer some solutions
as well. This is not as dire as
one can make it out to be. The mistake people make, and
actually it's interesting. It's a mistake that
Halsted made, is this idea that you just said. So there's a stage one
cancer, it then spreads through the lymph nodes, it
then moves into the next organ. That is not the biology
of cancer. The biology of cancer is that
some stage one tumors begin and they immediately metastasize or they immediately have
thrown out metastases. Halsted called this
the centrifugal theory, as you remember. And this is the basis for years
and years of radical surgery. He thought the more you cut
away, you'll cut away, you know, this centrifugally spreading --
like an inkblot spreading thing. You cut away the
edges of the inkblot, you've gotten rid of the tumor. That's not how cancer works. And it's very hard to
explain that to patients. The biology of the
tumor, its genetics, its relationship
with its environment. Its relationship with the rest
of your body is what determines that the quote, unquote,
"stage one tumor" that you've detected
by early detection. May have already spread way
outside a woman's breast if you're doing mammography
or colon if you're doing colonoscopy. Now, that's not completely true. There is a systematic spread,
but it's unpredictable. So therefore, the number of
people where you can find that systematic spread
and control it. It really depends on the
biology of the tumor. And it turns out that cervical
cancer is a good example. Cervical cancer tends to
spread in the centrifugal way. That's why pap smears work and
that's why you can cut it out. Colonoscopies work because
you can detect a pre-cancerous lesion before it has started
spreading into other places. Other tumors, you can't do that. So you can say, "Well,
oh my God, why go through all
this trouble?" Well, there's a way out of
this and it's an important way. We're beginning to explore it and I think we will
ultimately succeed. And it was taught to us by a
17th century mathematician. I keep saying screening
is a mathematical problem. And this was a very unusual
17th century mathematician named Thomas Bayes. And he wrote three papers of which one paper may be
the most important paper in statistics. And may even be the most
important paper in medicine. And Bayes made a
very simple argument. Bayes said, "Look, if you look
for a needle in a haystack, you'll find mostly hay and
not really many needles. If you put more needles
in the haystack, you'll start to find needles. If you put even further
needles in the haystack, you'll ultimately find
needles more often." >> Dhruv Khullar: I feel
like I could have come up with that [laughs]. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: I know. But it's astonishing that you --
well, one can come up with that. He actually came up with an
actual theorem that has to do with mathematics and you
can predict exactly what the number is. Anyway. But the bottom line, which is interesting
and very important. Is that with the tools of
risk assessment and especially with genetics, you can
do a Bayesian trial. And a Bayesian trial is very
different from a normal trial. A Bayesian trial is you
move the prior probability of someone being detected and
having a true positive cancer. A cancer that's lethal,
should be caught early. You move it now towards a
much more positive direction. Positive in the sense that you'll find more
needles rather than more hay. And you can now do that
with breast cancer. So that's why we screen
people with BRCA1 mutations. And we find cancers
in those patients. The trials are ongoing, but I suspect those are
life-saving decisions. So screening the
entire population. Screening in a 20-year-old
or in Dhruv Khullar, a scale three detected blip in
an MRI is unlikely to be cancer. In a 70-year-old person whose
father died of prostate cancer and whose uncle also had another
genetically related cancer. The chances that same lesion
is cancer is very likely to be true. So you have to have
a Bayesian approach. And in fact, I would say the
Bayesian approach is an approach to life. It's a way of living your life. It's a way of assessing risk. >> Dhruv Khullar: So
when the 30th edition of Emperor is released in 2040,
what will we be talking about? What will the next 15
years have brought us that, when I get to interview
you again at that time, we'll be going through then? How will cancers change? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: Well,
I think that the role of -- we talk a little bit about this. For so long, the theory of
cancer was dominated by cancer as a series of malignant
cells in a vacuum. That we forgot that these
cells actually interact with their environment. They live in an immune
environment. They bring out blood
vessels from the body. They live in an environment which is crucial
to their growth. And that's why some tumors
never grow while others grow. So we'll be talking about
kind of a more holistic view of what cancer is as a
disease, of that physiology. And hopefully we'll be
talking about medicines that either prevent, treat, or
detect based on that physiology. I think that's what
we'll be talking about. And we'll be talking about it, I
don't think just metaphorically, although the metaphors
are important. We'll be talking about it in
terms of actual medicines, some of which already
existing in the world. That really address this
immunological aspect, the micro-environmental aspect,
the metabolic aspects of cancer. Things that we knew about
but sort of ignored. >> Dhruv Khullar: You know, not
a lot of people who are doctors and academics and
successful writers end up becoming entrepreneurs as
well and starting businesses. What is the work that
you're doing trying to find new medicines using
artificial intelligence? And how does that play into
what we're talking about now? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: Well, so as you know I've
been very involved in finding new medicines. This is not my first time that
I've gone out of the clinic into finding new medicines. We were the first
group actually ever to use CRISPR for
cancer treatment. And so when AI came along, I
began to really dream about, or imagine, a mechanism of finding new medicines
for cancer using AI. I'm not talking about
AI to look at human data across populations, stuff that
maybe people in your group do or maybe you do yourself. Which is also a very
good use of the tool. What we do at Manas, which
is a company I co-founded with Reid Hoffman
and Ujjwal Singh. What we try to do at Manas is to
reimagine the molecular world. So just to take one step back, the growth of a cancer is
driven by a mutated gene. Which is driving the growth,
altering the metabolism, changing the way a
cell is behaving. It's no longer a normal
cell, it's a malignant cell. What we do at Manas is --
I'll make you close your eyes for a second, and think
about these mutant genes, these driving proliferators. And what we do at
Manas, basically, is we use AI in many ways. But one way is to take
this mutant protein, the one that's driving
the growth. And break it up, as it were,
into its atomic components. So now you've got this -- you can imagine this room
as the mutant protein, and we're trying to block it. We're going to put
some huge balloon, as it were, to block this room. But it has to have a particular
shape and a particular size. And what we do is we try to reimagine this room
at an atomic scale. Ascribing every dot. Imagine now this room pixelated, and devolved into
its properties. So no longer do we care about whether there is
an arch there or not. But rather we care about
the properties of that arch. And we distill, we make the
machine learn the properties so that it can then
find mechanisms to find new medicines. So we're using AI in
a very particular way. You know, we've had
various names for it. But the way I like to describe
it is, it's a molecular oracle. We're trying to create
a molecular oracle. And then ask the
molecular oracle questions that you would ask an oracle. Which is, now that you've
distilled the properties and learned the properties. That's what the AI piece is. You've learned the
properties of something. In an oracular way, tell
me that, how you're going to create a medicine against it. And the medicines that
we're hoping to get, we're actually now finding. Whether they work or not in
humans is a long ways away. But the kinds of
chemicals that we're hoping to find is chemical
matter that's never been discovered before. Is new chemical matter. Because the old chemical
matter that we've discovered against cancer, you know,
works in small instances. But doesn't work often enough. >> Dhruv Khullar: So
someone who's been working to develop new medicines
before the advent of these new AI tools, how big
of a step-change advance is it? I mean, should we
expect in five, 10, 15 years that we have
many more oncology drugs? Or is it the case that
it's not quite like that? That you still have
these bottlenecks of human biology being complex
and clinical trials and so on? So, you know, how do you square
that with what we've experienced in the past to the future? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: Well,
so my general impression is that AI will be an
enormous accelerant. It has generally been
-- again, remember, we're not creating chatbots. We're working in a
different model of AI. We're working with a
foundation model of AI. We're creating an oracle. Now, oracles in AI, like
all oracles, depend on data. You have to train them. They have to learn. And one of the bottlenecks
is data in this space. Now, thankfully, about
15 to 20 years ago, a group of scientists led
by a woman in England. Made it compulsory that
if you solve the structure of a protein. If you figured out, as I
said, in some ways, again, to use this analogy, the
structure of this room. Your paper could only be
published if you deposited that structure in
a public database. And think about just
that advance. And all the journals agreed
that would be the case. So there is a massive public
database where enormous amounts of protein structure is stored. And so what we do is, one of
our jobs is to train the oracle to learn from that database. And the real question is, even with that massive public
database, is there enough data? I suspect there is, but
I can't tell for sure. We'll know soon, actually. We'll know soon, at least
for this atomic model. We'll know whether this is going
to be a work in progress or not. >> Dhruv Khullar: So I know
you're not training chatbots, you're training an oracle. But how far are we from a chatbot writing The
Emperor of All Maladies? Like, is that conceivable
in five or 10 years? The job of fantastic
nonfiction writers, actually chatbots could
do that reasonably well? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: So the
other day I did an experiment. In which I said, write about
-- something or the other, I don't remember what I
was saying, in the style of Siddhartha Mukherjee. And I was horrified. >> Dhruv Khullar: Because
it was so good or so bad? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
It was terrible. It was terrible, and
I was like, oh my God, if I ever even come close
to writing like this. I mean, I must sound like
some crazy person on the page. So I think I'm safe for now. [ Laughter ] But I'm not sure. Maybe they'll figure out
some clever algorithm. But it was so horrifyingly
bad that I felt kind of simultaneously relieved
and a little embarrassed. >> Dhruv Khullar: SidGPT. I have one more question. I think we are going to move
on to audience questions. So if you have any and
you want to jot them down and pass them along. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: We're going to read
the last few pages. >> Dhruv Khullar: Read one more
at the end of the book here. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: So
this is the last two pages of the book. I read the first two
pages of the new book, and then now the last two pages. So the first book,
many of you remember, ended with my patient Germaine. I think of my patient
Germaine often. I remember her sitting
with such perfect poise on the evening before she died. I recall her grace and dignity
and the enormity of her courage. I think of her will and
wit and imagination, and I also think
of her sly humor. "I know you're writing a
journal about your fellowship. I want to say good
luck," she told me. And then she winked. This voluminous book was once
no more than an untidy sheaf of notes that I carried. And she had once seen the
papers fall out of my backpack. Her voice was soft and flecked
with infinite kindness even as she strained to breathe. I felt moved to tears. Here was a woman
submerged in pain and fear, drenched in grief. And yet she had found time to
think about the scribblings of a lowly fellow
in scrubs leaning over her with a stethoscope. A group of nurses
entered the room to wheel the bed
next to her away. The bed was folded and moved
to the corridor outside. The patient had passed. "Whatever you write," she
said, "make me look fabulous. Fabulous, okay? I don't have much time." There was momentary
silence in the room. And the soft screech of
a bed being wheeled away. "What is time?" A student of a Zen monk asked
his master month after month. There was no reply,
just silence. A few months later, the
monk replied irritably, "Time is a head of cabbage." There are many variations
of this koan. But the point, perhaps, is
that time is inscrutable. It lies beyond human
comprehension, and asking questions
about time doesn't make it more understandable. The question is absurd,
and so is its answer. The time I spent with
Germaine was one evening. Or was it a few months,
or is it a lifetime? Time was a hospital bed
that would be folded and stowed away in some corner. Perhaps it was an unforgettable
evening in a room spent talking about a farm in her
hometown in Alabama. Or perhaps a lifetime
thinking about a patient that I still cannot forget. With a shape-shifting,
inscrutable illness that I'm still trying
to understand. Time is a head of cabbage. I've spent my life in
search of comprehension, but it still eludes me. But at least in that searching, I discovered a cosmos
of realities. A few months after
Emperor was published, I received a handwritten letter. It was from Germaine's brother. Even though I changed her name
and disguised her identity, he had recognized
her in my book. "She would have loved, admired, and understood your
book," he wrote. Understood. A strange word. A word that signals a desire to
know, not just about patients, scientists, and doctors. But about history, about
the birth and growth and future of a discipline. That evening, somehow,
Germaine had intuited that Emperor would be much
more than a personal journal. She understood what I
wanted to understand. This was the best praise I've
ever received about this book. Thank you. [ Applause ] >> Dhruv Khullar: Now you
know why AI can't emulate Sid Mukherjee. We have a couple questions here. And I'm guessing that people
are more accustomed to typing than writing longhand now, because it's very
difficult to parse this. But can you talk
about how you write? What is your daily process
and what tools do you use? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: So I
write in a very bizarre way. That's been written
about humorously. I write in bed. Almost exclusively. I find it very comforting. And when I had a new office,
I put a bed in my office. I suspect that that would
not be allowed today. [ Laughter ] But then I quickly removed it. But how do I write? I write in small bursts. I write when I have a thought. I was just reading this piece on Oliver Sacks today
in the New Yorker. A lovely piece. And he wrote compulsively, and
he kept journals after journals. And he wrote and he wrote. And he would sit in a
taxi and write, et cetera. I write anti-compulsively. And that means that I think
about what I'm going to write, but I don't put a
single word down. And I think for a long
time, and I keep thinking. And then, when I'm
hopefully in bed, I can write a full
chapter in one go. And I write from
beginning to end. I edit, or Nan really
helps me edit later. But I write from the first
word to the last word. Because in some ways
I've been thinking about the process on the way. >> Dhruv Khullar:
What concerns you most about our current Department
of Health and Human Services? [ Laughter ] >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Where does one start? Look, you know -- [ Laughter ] -- let me talk a little
bit about the rhetoric of anti-science, which is
probably what's driving. I don't have a personal
animosity against any particular
person in the Department of Health and Human Services. Except that there's one person
that I'd love to vaccinate. [ Laughter ] But that said, you know, I think that the concerns
are very broad. The concerns are
that since the 1950s, the United States has led
the world in innovation. And this is innovation
in every way. Cultural innovation,
scientific innovation, technological innovation. This is the one thing
we're actually good at. We're terrible at many things. But this is the one thing that
has always been the centerpiece of how we have come
to change the world. And I think that the
anti-science rhetoric and the anti-culture rhetoric. The anti-reading rhetoric,
and the fake news rhetoric. Is one way that we will
destroy this amazing lead. So, you know, we can
talk about particulars and why the HHS has
so many problems. But I want to talk
about the generalities that we're destroying. We're actively seeing
the dismantling of a particular kind
of civilization. And the fact that we're actively
watching it and doing nothing about it should be
deeply embarrassing to us. >> Dhruv Khullar: Does it
change the way you think about communicating? You know, in an anti-elite,
anti-expertise, anti-science type
of environment. When so many people are
skeptical and distrustful of institutions and academics
and doctors and so on. How should people in
positions of influence and power communicate
about these issues? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Well, I mean, to some extent, I think doctors and physicians and scientists painted
themselves into a corner by becoming experts
without humility. It's very easy to dismantle an
expert because an expert belongs to a class of the elite. And, even though it's being
governed by the elite, ironically, this is a
very anti-elitist moment. So I think scientists,
physicians, and in their rhetoric,
in the use of language, in the use of power, really
painted ourselves into a corner. Because we assume the role
of the language of the elite without assuming the
humility that comes with being that elite. I mean, you don't
need me to tell you that we don't know half
a thing about cancer. Otherwise, we'd be
treating and curing it with much more vigor
than we are today. So I think the answer to your
question is for ourselves to go back and describe
the process by which we come
to understanding. Which is a messy,
complex, often a process which does not involve the
kind of scientific heroism. Which is so easy to
disband, you know? The scientific hero begs
the scientific antihero. The pro-vaccinator begs
the anti-vaccinator. Because the anti-vaccinator
says, "Well, wait a second, how did you get to
be the boss of me?" So the answer to the question of how we communicate
during this time is to climb down from the various
towers that we occupy. And speak to people
in the language that we ourselves
speak with each other. Which is a language that
involves not knowing, language that involves humility. But also language that involves
an attempt to understand. You know, to be honest, we actually don't
know all the myriad -- although it was an incredible
savior of human lives. We don't know all
the myriad effects that the COVID vaccine
has had on human beings. Admit it. It's okay. You know, civilization
will not crumble if you or I as physicians say that
we save millions of lives. And yet we don't know
some fundamental things about immunology in vaccination. It's okay. And it's precisely this kind
of oppositional, technocratic, befuddling way of speaking. And the lack of communication
that's led to this moment. And we have to undo it. >> Dhruv Khullar: Can
cancer ever be fully cured or just managed? Is curing it a pipe dream? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: It
depends on the kind of cancer. I think we're curing lots,
and we are going to cure more. We are curing childhood
leukemias at 80%, 90% of cases of childhood leukemias. Have long-term cures,
terrible side effects. We're trying to now
work backwards to diminish those side effects. A woman with breast
cancer living in 2025 has a completely
different outlook on her life than a woman with breast
cancer in 2015 or even in 1915. You know, massive changes. A woman or a man with pancreatic or gallbladder cancer
that's spread has no change in their life expectancy,
basically, give or take, since dozens of years. So I think we will find a way
through, find a pathway through. And every breakthrough will
be a great moment for us as scientists, as human
beings, and as patients. But it's very hard to
answer, "Can we ever cure it?" It's not going away,
that's for sure. We know that random, stochastic
genetic accidents cause cancer, just as carcinogens
cause cancer. We know exactly how
they cause cancer. They cause mutations, and
those happen when cells divide. When they make copies
of themselves. You can get a random
genetic accident. And that is the genetic accident
that leads to cancer, among, you know, many other things. So I think the word
cancer is not going to be removed from our society. In the way that, you
know, I don't know, certain other diseases
have been really -- in the way that smallpox,
you know, has really vastly,
not completely. But vastly disappeared
from our society. So cancer will always
be with us. Its metaphors will be with
us, its memes will be with us. But I think we'll change
the way we think about it. >> Dhruv Khullar: We
have a couple questions from young people in the
audience, it looks like. And I guess, putting them
together, what advice you have for people who are at the
start of their careers? Who want to do something
in medicine, or science, or journalism, or who just
want to nudge the world in a better direction? >> Siddhartha Mukherjee: Well -- -- I have a memory of a young
journalist and doctor who came to my office 10 years
ago, 15 years ago. And that was Dhruv. And I don't remember
-- generally speaking, I give terrible advice. So whatever I'm going to say, you should probably
immediately discount it. But the general thing that
I say to people is to have at least one big idea. And then once you know you have
that big idea, really follow it. It sounds like vague
advice, and it probably is. I'm not going to give you
a step-by-step, you know, ascendancy into becoming a
writer for The New Yorker. But nonetheless, I do
think it's important to have one big idea
and to execute it. And really to learn
how to execute it. To really put yourself --
to learn how to execute it. I'll give you a quick anecdote
which I found very moving. I went to a medical conference. And, you know, medical
conferences have scientists. And they have physicians and
physician scientists, et cetera. And I went to a dinner, and
someone had passed away, and it was a memorial. And the person stood up and
said, you know, so on, so forth, whoever it was, never made a
major scientific discovery. But he learned how to
cure childhood leukemia. And I thought to myself, "That's
a very beautiful thought." He never made a big
scientific discovery, but maybe he learned
how to speak to patients and their families. He learned how to bring the
child back year after year, month after month,
to be reexamined. He learned how to administer
chemotherapy in a way that the child would
be able to take it. He maybe learned
how to tell a story. Maybe he learned puppetry. I mean, I don't know,
but he learned something. He had one big idea, and
he learned something. So I don't think there's a
formulaic way of stating it. But to learn how to do
something is very beautiful and very important. And now we have machines
to compete with, so we'd better do it quickly. >> Dhruv Khullar: Well, Sid,
thank you for this conversation. Thank you for this
book, and thank you all for coming this evening. >> Siddhartha Mukherjee:
Thank you. [ Applause ]

cancer is as old as human life itself.
The first known written reference to cancer appears in a 15-foot papyrus
prepared by an Egyptian physician 4,000 years ago. He numbered all the diseases
and their treatments known to the ancient world. Case number 45 refers to
swellings of the breasts, large spreading and hard. Under the section titled
treatment, it reads simply, "there is none." The main impetus for for making the book and the documentary was was to sort of give us a road map of where we are and
where we're going with cancer research I'd like to describe the documentary as
a kind of report card on cancer and it's it's not just documentary I think of it
as news in some ways you know in our long and complex struggle against this
family of diseases called cancer we've really not had a report card, we haven't
had a time in which we've sort of taken a step back and said let's create a way
to think about what's been achieved, what the challenges are, what the
next steps are in a very global sense all the way from the basic science to
the delivery of extremely complex new innovative cancer care to patients. We
have an incredible series of resources here at Columbia that are really
unmatched around the world on one hand we have a community hospital where
patients really come off the large community that we have around the campus
but also we have a really a state-of-the-art academic medical center
where the capacity to collaborate with leaders in the field of lymphoma in the
field of leukemia in the biology of multiple diseases, multiple forms of
cancer, is really unmatched. There's not one kind of cancer but there are many
kinds of cancers and that even within let's say a particular specimen of
of you let's say just take breast cancer or even take her 2 positive breast cancer.
One woman's breast cancer in the genetic sense is different from another woman's
breast cancer, even though they might share some common features. So one of the large projects that's going on right now is a project of determining to what
extent do different cancers share common features and to what extent are they
really different. Cancer is not a disease which is a just a cell in isolation but
in fact has a physiological context and to understand cancer you need to
understand its physiological context which means collaborating with people
who don't necessarily work on cancer and to treat cancer you need similarly a
collaborative context in which you are asking for help from people in kidney
diseases from people who from doctors who take care of bone diseases from
doctors who are in the neurological center so I think this is this kind of
collaboration is very unique and it really plays to the strengths of the
departments. You can imagine an end to cancer and the answer is probably no but
a thoughtful no I think we'll prevent some cancers we will treat some cancers, we will cure some other cancers, and some will remain challenges and the diversity
of our approach to the diversity of cancer will reflect the complex biology
of this disease

Thank you so much. It's a real honor to speak to all of you today. I'll have a provocative title to my talk. I've called it genomics in the post genome era. And I have a very particular meaning when I talk about this. I grew up in the I would say the genomics era, but I miss I missed it. I haven't sequenced many genomes. I my laboratory doesn't do a lot of sequencing. But what we do is use the information that's been made available by genomics and try to convert that into human therapies. So we sit on the shoulders of giants essentially. But we've never we don't actively explore. There was a time especially in oncology cancer, which is the discipline that I that I practice. There was a time where there was a there was a kind of sobering moment in oncology when the human genome was sequenced and following that cancer genomes began to be sequenced. The the the pre-sobering moment, the hype was that the sequencing of these genomes would reveal immediately would reveal a whole bunch of therapies for cancers. And in fact revealed the mechanisms by which cancers proliferated, grew, and metastasized. And then came the disappointment, which was that they that the that the genome didn't reveal a lot of information. It revealed some information, but not a lot of information especially in the therapeutic front. And that disappointment continues today to some extent. So for instance, when I the the disease that I treat mostly, lymphomas, leukemias, especially acute myelogenous leukemia, um you know, the genes that are involved in uh the mutated genes found in AML are genes that have to do with uh methylation, RNA splicing, things that you wouldn't imagine that you wouldn't imagine at the first glance would immediately tell you why these genes are involved in in that particular kind of blood cancer. And yet they are. We know clearly they are. And in fact, it's an ongoing investigation in my lab and other laboratories to figure out how these genes that seem to be unrelated at the first glance, uh you know, they're not for instance genes that regulate the cell cycle. They're not genes that regulate at the first glance, um you know, the typical proliferative capacities of a cell. But somehow or the other, there's a message that we're missing um in cellular biology um that that was not solved. Um so, there was a disappointment that came with that. But now we're realizing that we can we as I said in this what I would call the post-genome era, we're finding new ways to treat and think about cancer in particular, which I think are are profound and interesting, and they all rely on the information released by um by the by the sequencing of genomes. So, I'll focus on that. I'll focus on physiology and focus on cancer therapeutics. Uh and um So, I I suppose that the um the the the central theme of my talk is this, which is that um the news of of of my death has been vastly exaggerated. Uh sometimes attributed to Twain. Um, but I was also thinking about how we might be able to treat and cure a a very lethal cancer which is really whose needle has not moved in the last 50, 60 odd years and that's acute myeloid leukemia. Especially relapsed refractory acute myeloid leukemia where the mortality rate rivals any of the worst cancers that you can imagine including pancreatic and other forms of of extraordinarily lethal solid tumors. The problem is that um, hematopoietic stem cells and progenitor cells, the cells that give rise to the myeloid cells in our body, the lymphoid cells in our body, but importantly the myeloid cells in our body, neutrophils, macrophages, red blood cells, platelets, etc., etc. And AML cells share common antigens and to my mind until now for AML and not for other diseases we have never found a unique tumor antigen that we can target. Um, I'll talk about why that's different from another disease that I work on, ALL, acute lymphoblastic leukemia. AML, we haven't had a a unique tumor antigen. Every time we've targeted anything in a acute myelogenous leukemia cell, it's turned out to have its counterpart in some myeloid cell. And therefore you get dose limiting toxicities constantly and you get a you reach a ceiling, you reach a limit and then you can't go any further because the more you treat the patient, the more patient they they they the more toxicities you accumulate. Um and so that ceiling is almost a natural ceiling. It's been true for virtually every drug that's been tested, every small molecule, every targeted therapy that's been tested in this space. And so we've in in some ways we've we've really not moved the needle um for this uh particular cancer. So, this collaboration is the idea of using a monocyte as an effector cell. So, remember that CAR-T cells have been used for a long time, genetically modified T cells, CAR-T 33 in our case and other CAR-Ts. I'll talk about those in a second, but but the question was why don't we use monocytes? The problem with monocytes, as you know, is that they're part of the innate immune system and they don't have a adaptive capacity, so they're not specific for any antigen or cancer. So, we said, "Well, what if we made them specific for an antigen or a cancer?" So, just a background, myeloid cells are the most abundant cells in solid tumors. They have tumoricidal activity. We know that from in vitro and in vivo experiments, but they don't have specificity, right? So, the question was how can you impart specificity to a myeloid cell? Um so, the um what this allows us to do, of course, is not what I'm particularly proud of is to treat and cure patients uh with relapse refractory ALL and myeloma. It allows us to do a completely novel clinical trial design, which I am most excited about. Cuz if you bring COGS down by 10-fold or 20-fold or 30-fold, um you can do a completely novel kind of trial in which you say, "Well, instead of waiting for someone to fail three lines of therapy, and then give the CD19 or anti-myeloma CAR-T, what if we gave them, because we can afford to, what if we gave them the therapy at first remission?" Um a completely different idea and and I'm very very interested in this trial because I'm the the one thing I've learned in oncology, which I think is I learned from Bert Vogelstein the first time I met with Bert is Bert told me that there are three zeros between a million and a billion. Um and bizarrely enough, oncologists have forgotten that there are three zeros between a million and a billion. The relevance of that is if you have a billion cells that you're treating, it has a billion possibilities of escaping from your therapy. If you have a million cells you're treating, that is reduced by a factor of three logs. If you have a thousand cells you're treating, that is reduced by a factor of three logs. We seem to have forgotten this idea, but of course this is the basis of adjuvant therapy or in in in oncology in general is that if you treat a slower disease burden, you also get the double advantage not only of treating less cancer and more and and more on target cancer treatment, but you also get the advantage that you have less chances of relapse and failure of treatment cuz you have log fold differences in the capacity of the cells to relapse. The problem is that we can't use this because we're using typically we're using drugs in the third line, fourth line, sixth line, eighth line setting and by that time you have that carrier type that I showed you in the AML patient. Every single chromosome is gone awack, mutations galore, etc. etc. And of course you increase the capacity of the tumor to escape. Um by moving cogs down, you have really the capacity now to do what is quite I think a revolutionary kind of study, which is a a new trial design. So, I'm not going to give you all the data because these are two abstracts present We have six abstracts at ASH, uh the American Society of Hematology meeting. This is This trial is called Imagine. And then we argued or we hypothesized that what was happening is that the inhibition of PI3 kinase we and again, this shows you the blind spots that we have as as tumor biologists. We think of the tumor as if it's sitting in space. But of course, it's not sitting in space. It's sitting inside an animal. It's sitting inside a human. And so, what happens inside a human is that yes, the PI3 kinase is inhibiting the uh the inhibitor, the drug is inhibiting the uh uh PI3 kinase in the tumor, but it must also have physiological effects because there's a whole animal. It's not just sitting in space. And in fact, the physiological effect is now the release of the the body, this is the hypothesis, the body thinks that it's going through starvation because you've inhibited the central detector of of nutrients. And the response of the body is essentially your liver starts releasing glucose. That glucose then prompts the pancreas to release insulin. And now the hypothesis was that that insulin reactivates um the very genes or the very downstream pathways that the PI3K inhibitor was supposed to inhibit. I liken this to the woodcutter the famous fable of the woodcutter who sits on the tree and chops off the limb that he or she is is sitting on. Um and in fact, that turned out to be true. The most The simplest way to see this is the following. This is a tumor imaged by PET um either given vehicle or a PI3K inhibitor BKM120. And you would imagine since PET detects the glucose flux into the tumor, you'd imagine that in fact if the tumor was dying, you'd imagine the glucose flux would go down. In fact, just the opposite happens and we've done this in humans now as well. So in fact, just the opposite happens. In fact, the glucose flux goes up. Um and what really happens within the tumor is a won't go through a lot of the details, but in in in summary, what happens is what I just told you is that you get a uh uh glucose release into the pancreas. Pancreas then releases insulin. The insulin now we know binds to the insulin-like growth factor receptor and that receptor reactivates multiple genes that are resistance mechanisms to PI3K. We've shown this now in multiple models of cancer, I think 12 models of cancer. And what's interesting is that it's mutation agnostic. It's true for PI3K mutated tumors, but it's also true for PI3K in its non-mutated tumors, including pancreatic cancers in the mouse. I think I have the mouse data here. This is just that same idea repeated, which is to say that the inhibitor certainly inhibits PI3K alpha in the tumor cells. This is in the case of AML. Uh and thereby inhibits AKT, S6, and mTOR in the tumor. But the inhibitor also inhibits in the pancreas and the liver PI3K, thereby releasing insulin acting through the insulin receptor, and reactivating the downstream genes involved in tumor proliferation, resistance, and growth. Um So Um this is the There are two strategies by which you can lower insulin in vivo. You can use drugs or you can use a ketogenic diet. I remember this ketogenic diet, it's an insulin-suppressive diet. There are 1,000 variations of ketogenic diets. The sole role here is to suppress insulin. It's not to make ketones. Ketones are only there so that you can live. The sole goal here is to reduce insulin. And we we've done it done variations on this theme. We can use SGLT inhibitors or the ketogenic diet. It turns out that actually the diet plus drug combination is the best. This is the lowering of insulin in in with the ketogenic diet alone. And you can see that if you do this combination therapy, you can see that the insulin receptors is not activated, and there's a lot of biochemistry in the paper that shows that these downstream things from the insulin receptor, including AKT, S6, and mTOR are no longer activated. Um Um this is the mouse data. This is AML. Uh, it's true for uh, allografts of pancreatic cancer. It's true for endometrial cancer. Uh, these are PDX models. I think we've taken I think about 12 um, such independent models um, and shown that you get very profound reductions in uh, and in very profound uh, effects on the tumor if you combine an insulin lowering strategy with the uh, PI3K kinase alpha inhibitors. A mass extinction is a is a very simple concept. Um it's just considered a a period of time, a short period of time, relatively short on a geological scale uh when a lot of the diversity of the planet, for whatever reason, crashes and um this idea that there's a sixth extinction obviously implies there've have five previous ones. There have been, lo and behold, the first occurred 440 million years ago during a period known as the Ordovician period when when life was still largely confined to the water. So, about 3/4 of all marine species died out and very few on land cuz there were very few on land. And the most familiar one of these mass extinctions is the fifth, um which did in the dinosaurs 66 million years ago. And there's a pretty broad scientific consensus now uh that that one was caused by an asteroid. And for a long time people tried to find common themes in these extinctions, you know, is there one reason why they happen? They happen at irregular periods. Um and no one has found yet, you know, the key, what causes a mass extinction. Um but the general sort of theme of them is that the world changes in some way faster than evolution can keep up with, right? So, so it all does boil down to genetics. Um so if I'm a you know, a worm or a plant, I can only keep up with environmental change by either I have enough plasticity, you know, I have enough adaptability. If I if I can't go here, I go there. If I can't use this food source, I go that food source. So, all organisms have a certain amount of plasticity. Um but the other way I adapt by my make a big adaptation is is is through, you know, mutations, through genetic through evolution, right? I evolve. I become a new creature. And that's, you know, propelled life throughout the last 3 and 1/2, you know, billion years or so. So, if things change really fast and I don't have the plasticity and I can't evolve fast enough, uh the end is near. That's that's the end of the line uh for my species. And what are we humans doing? Uh we've sort of transcended evolution in a really really important sense. And this gets to a lot of the things that Sid was talking about. We don't wait around to evolve, right? If we if we have a new pathogen, when you go to get, you know, a flu shot, right? That flu evolved into a new virus, but you got the vaccine and you skipped that evolutionary step, right? So, the flu didn't just kill off everyone who wasn't immune to it. We jumped over that. And that's and that's why we've we've done so well, why we've extended our own lives, why we're we're so successful. But the flip side of that are these massive changes that we are making to the planet on a on a geological scale. I'm sure everyone in this room has heard this concept that we're in a new geological epoch, the Anthropocene. An epoch is actually to a geologist a very small amount of time. There have been many people who believe we're in a new geological period. So, the last we're, you know, we're in the um What are we in? The Neogene or something like that? But we we we may be in a whole new period of time, a very big geological divide because we have so we're changing the planet on such a scale and such at such a rate and at such a scale. So, I'm just going to throw out a couple of the ways we're doing that. Unfortunately, I could spend all night talking about how we're doing that, but I I will not. I will turn this over to a conversation. We're changing the climate really fast. We're putting up CO2 really, really fast into the atmosphere, perhaps as fast as, you know, has ever happened in Earth's history. um And when you think about it, it makes sense. It's really hard to put CO2 into the air, right? So, all the fossil fuels that we are burning are just carbon that a plant absorbed during its lifetime. The plant died and for complicated geological reasons didn't decompose, but became a fossil fuel. That process took hundreds of millions of years and we are now digging up those stores of fossil fuels. We're burning them, let's say, in a matter of a couple of centuries. So, that's really, really rapid and if people weren't here, how could that be happening? Uh we're acidifying the oceans. That same CO2 that we're throwing up into the air that's changing the climate is changing the chemistry of the oceans. Um we are very rapidly altering the surface of the earth when we go in, you know, to the Amazon or or you know, when people came to the US and mowed down the prairies and planted corn. That's a monoculture. That's a place where very, very few um creatures can survive. Everyone's heard about, you know, monarchs are crashing monarch butterfly populations. That's because, you know, we go in to what was used to be their habitat. We just mow it down, put down herbicides, plant corn and there's just nothing for them to eat. It's as simple as that. Um other ways, we are moving things around the world. This one doesn't get as much attention as it probably should. Um but, you know, over the vast span of evolutionary time, uh you know, the continents have divided. Um all the continents used to be squished together um about 250 million years ago into a continent called we call Pangea. Then the continents split apart. The world as we know it came into being. Evolutionary lineages have been evolving separately for tens of millions of years and then we bring them together, right? You got a you know, you got a Africa after someone from Africa comes here. Um you know, all you got to Asia, vice versa. We're moving, we're moving goods around, we're moving people around, we're moving viruses around, we're moving fungi around and just to give one really local example of what happens uh is people have probably heard of a white nose syndrome killing bats all across the US. That was a fungal disease, a fungus brought over from Europe, probably totally unwittingly by someone that's just killed um millions and millions of bats. So, often times um when people try to date when did the anthropocene begin, uh they use one date, you know, that is used as a beginning of the use of fossil fuels, and another is post-war when human population really, really took off. Um so, all of these questions of what what we're going to do, you know, to enhance our own lives, um unfortunately, are coming at the expense of all these other species with whom we share the planet. The the famous the famous conundrum which has been raised many times by philosophers since a long time. If you have a boat and you replace every plank in the boat, is the boat still the boat? The story begins in a strange place. It doesn't begin in a laboratory. It doesn't begin in an university. It doesn't begin in a standard place that you might imagine the most consequential and I would argue the most dangerous idea in the history of in the recent history of science came from. It begins in a monastery. It begins in a monastery. I I spent time here. I spent a week and a half here 6 years ago in a small town called Brno. And that's where in this walled garden a small area in this room over here, this corner room overlooking the walled garden a man named Gregor Mendel began to perform some experiments. He was a monk. He had been rejected multiple times from university education. He was a failed biologist, which is should give us all a lot of reassurance that you can essentially write the most important paper in the history of biological sciences having failed your examinations in biology. But here he was. He was a basically he was a monk and he was a substitute teacher. And he began he began to experiment. We think, although we're not sure, in this little plot of land with which is weirdly enough planted with gardenias. Um But anyway, but he of course Mendel began to plant peas. Why you would take Mendel's garden and put gardenias in it, I don't know, but anyway. Um but Mendel began to plant peas and he made in 1864, 1865 an incredibly important realization. And that realization was that heredity the fact that you and I resemble our parents somewhat um that is carried not by some kind of miasmic substance, some kind of peculiar weirdness but in fact is carried in a discrete form like information moving from your parents to the offspring, from your parents to you in a kind of discrete informational form like words in a sentence. You can't tear those words apart, they will lose meaning. You can't scatter the letters apart, they will lose meaning. Like the word word it is inherited in fullness from your parents to your offspring, to the children. And Mendel described all of this just by looking at in great detail by looking at peas. And it had many consequences. He realized that in every generation information in this discrete form not in the kind of blended form is moving from one generation to the next. Now, just to contrast this with a very simple idea which existed before Mendel, people thought that your nose or your eyes are some kind of intermediate between your father's and your mother's nose and eyes. And this was human intuition for generations that we were the products of some kind of blending that the genetic information was taken from your father and you from your mother and blended in some kind of biological blender and your nose came out of that. It's a very intuitive idea. It makes a lot of sense. Except of course it has one big problem. It takes you 2 seconds to figure out what that problem is. And it's called gender. Right? So, it couldn't possibly be true that in every generation there's some kind of blending going on because otherwise our anatomies would become genderless. So, there has to be a reason that you and I are inheriting information from our parents that is somehow that is somehow kept generation upon generation intact. And it's astonishing that would take Mendel in 1864 several thousand years after many of the fundamental tenets of biology had been discovered to essentially rediscover this idea that of course that information, hereditary information, the thing that makes us that gives us our anatomy, our physiology, our traits, our our and by extension our identities is not being blended in some kind of biological blender, but is carried out in discrete in this form that reproduces itself generation upon generation. That's the only way you can explain how gender, male, female remain anatomically, physiologically remain uh intact as it were generation upon generation. There's no other simple explanation for that. What's interesting to me about that story about the story of the discovery of genes as a fundamental unit is that soon after Mendel had discovered this biologists who worked on genes in the early 1900s were speeding forward a little bit. In the early 1900s began to realize that this was a powerful and dangerous idea long before they knew what it was. What carried this information? How was this information transmitted? What was its relevance to diseases like cancer or metabolic diseases or diabetes? What was its relevance to of this information that moved from parent to offspring long before we knew all of this. The movement to use that information to discriminate between human beings, to make to to identify those who were weak versus those who were strong already was being practiced. Here a couple of examples of that. It's interesting that the first the most powerful examples of what we call eugenics, the idea of using genes to understand and thereby the hope to create a better society by using genetics was actually born very soon after Mendel. It was born in the UK. It was born in England and led really by a man who coined the word eugenics itself, Francis Galton. Galton was a cousin of Darwin's. And while Darwin was thinking about evolution Galton was thinking about social evolution. How can we make better societies? And of course one way to make a better better society he thought was by selecting selectively breeding the best together. By selectively breeding the people who were the best adapted, the best looking the the the the the brightest, the smartest. These were Galton Darwin's own ideas. Um so, this was he this was so called positive eugenics, the idea that you would use this feature, the selective breeding of human beings to create better humans, better societies, better races, better countries. Um The idea then morphs and comes to the United States. Now, recall that we haven't even spoken about Germany yet. Um in the 1910s and 1920s where these kinds of advertisements are popular. You would open up magazine and see something like this. And in the United States it morphs into a kind of practical realm. In the United States it becomes moves from selective breeding to selective sterilization. Why don't we sterilize women or men, but of course women predominantly, who are likely to carry defective traits into the next generation and thereby remove their influence from future societies. This is Carrie Buck. My book is dedicated to Carrie Buck. I would encourage everyone to read that history. The history is very pertinent to this state as well. Carrie Buck was one of the first women to be sterilized under this law of trying to make sure that the best that that people who were unfit to breed were sterilized. The case climbed to the Supreme Court and there was a judgment passed down which is very chilling. It says three generations of imbeciles is enough and Carrie Buck was forcibly sterilized to prevent the prevent the birth of what was then considered genetically defective mentally ill children. It turns out in retrospect that in fact Carrie Buck had really no illness. She was just one of us. But it's a it's it's a reminder of how quickly from 1864 to 1920, barely a flash of time, that the idea of heredity, of genetics, of inherency was being moved from an abstraction, something that someone was planting a monk was planting in peas, to an idea that was used as a as as of social and biological control. Here, of course, is that example of that. Um it is only after all of this and remember we still don't know what the carrier of this information is, who's carrying this information, where this information comes from. Is it a chemical? Is it a substance? What is it? We don't know any of this. We're in the 1920s, 1930s that that same idea morphs from eugenics as uh a mechanism of breeding the most uh the best and the brightest as Galton would have it in the in England to eugenics as a uh sterilization effort against uh women who carried uh what was thought to be genes of imbecility and other weaknesses. Um and ultimately it morphs to its most macabre form, which is the form that we remember it today, which is uh Nazi Germany. Here are uh here's a Nazi physician measuring twins. Here is um actually one of the most interesting images that I found while working on the book. Um um SS cadets, you can see a swastika um in this man's hand um watching on the blackboard. And if you look in the blackboard, um the man who is the um a captain of the Nazi uh party um has Mendel's laws and Mendel's uh uh diagrams up on the blackboard. Um here is from the United States a better babies contest. Some of you might remember these better babies contests. Um here are babies being judged for being the most eugenically fit. Um they are being judged for future the idea that you would create a future race where this would now become um racially cleansed and therefore superior um as a mechanism of social control. Barely, as I said, 40 years after the discovery of the idea of the gene itself. One of the consequences of the war was that it brought a sense of shame to this project. A very important sense of shame to this project. Um an embarrassment because of the Nazi excess of it. But what what one of the things I want to talk about is that it never fully decapitated the desire. The desire to improve ourselves, the desire to improve our societies, desire to improve our own children through control of heredity is an ancient desire. And that's why I asked you those first few questions to ask ourselves about what we really desire. What do we desire? What kinds of things are we going to allow our societies uh to move into um as we move forward into understanding more and more about ourselves. Keep that thought in mind. So, I'm going to go back into the history. I'm going to bring you up to to um quickly. I'm going to bring you up to where we are today and then go back into these questions that I had just asked you before. So, um the explicit eugenic desires of the 1920s and 1930s were contained partly because of the excesses of what happened in Germany in the 1930s and 1940s, the extermination of human beings based on eugenics. This the the final that the the progression from better breeding to sterilization to extermination was a was of course a step way into the abyss of eugenics. And by the 1940s, we had decided as societies to keep aside those questions, move them away, and instead concentrate on what was it that was transmitting this information across generations. And that, of course, turned out to be a molecule that was very unknown to most biologists called DNA. If you said the word DNA in the 1920s and 1930s, if said you were working on DNA, people would say, "What a weird thing to work on." Um it is a strange molecule, not interesting, probably some structural component of uh the human body. Who would have thought that this would be the carrier of information? But in fact, this is a picture of Rosalind Franklin, whose birthday we celebrated a few months ago, um looking down a microscope and taking photographs of the substance that she and others by that time had known and had established was in fact the molecule that Mendel had identified. Mendel didn't know any about the molecules, but the but the substance that lay behind uh Mendel's uh idea that heredity was transmitted across generations. And the final structure of that molecule was solved in the 1950s by Jim Watson and Francis Crick. And here is that first model of that molecule, um a double helix. Um all of us contain every cell in our bodies contains this double helix. If this double helix was in a single cell was expanded out it would be it's and and put all together in a long chain, it would be several meters long. And each cell of our body contains this information, this twisted double helix, and condenses it many fold, almost a million fold, into that cell. And that information tells the cell how to behave, what to be how to act, and it gets transmitted from us to our children through sperm and eggs. And that is of course the secret behind the transmission of information from one generation to the next. Now, if I took your um genome uh it would contain this double helix, obviously in this double helix form, this chemical form. It would contain 3 billion letters of uh of DNA written ACTG. And if you think about that, if it was standardly printed on an encyclopedia, it would be about 70 full sets of the Encyclopedia Britannica. One human being's genome. So, all the walls you can imagine in this um in this room would be lined with your genome. And if I wanted to move to your genome, I would have to make another auditorium this size and line all the walls again with the encyclopedia of your genome and so forth. So, each of one each one of us carries within our bodies one such 70-set Encyclopedia Britannica. Now, we knew this, we began to understand this, and we also began to understand that you could pick out one volume, one page, and it would be inscrutable to us in the 1960s, 1950s. It would read ACTG CCTG and so forth. We would have no meaning behind all of this. But how does this encyclopedia specify us? How does it specify us in our uh our the the structure of our body, our forms, our cells? Well, the simple answer is that it makes human beings uh it makes animals, organisms by creating messages, and those messages ultimately uh become functional. Those messages become actualized. And um work done by several other people, this is work done by Marshall Nirenberg, we learned to read the how this information we began to learn to read how this information actually gets actualized. And by the 1950s and 1960s, again, this idea that DNA makes a message, makes a protein um was was made obvious. There was one last thing we need to know, and that's important, and that is that this is a dynamic process. Here's the best example of the dynamism of this process. This creature which looks nothing like this creature, both of these have the same DNA. Their information is exactly identical. So, somehow or the other in this state the genome of this um caterpillar reads out caterpillar and within the within the matter of several days, um several hours, that same genome is read out to make a butterfly. And if you understand this, you begin to understand how you can have thousands of different cell types in your body, but every cell carries the its own genome. Your liver cell, your brain cells, your nerve cells, your blood cells all carry the same genome. They interact, this genome sort of expresses itself, this encyclopedia makes different versions of itself. It actualizes itself differently in different cells and different organs, and that is called gene regulation. So, by the 1950s and 1960s, we had realized all of this, that there was a master encyclopedia that we carried in our cells. This master encyclopedia uh allowed the creation of very different forms by reading out subparts of it. But in fact, despite that, the that the master message, the encyclopedia remained the same. But the question was there were two questions, could we read the actual letters? And number two, could we change the actual letters? And this already had many many consequences. I'll give you the simplest consequences. Take a medicine like insulin. A medicine like insulin, you it's very hard to extract. You can move genes across the um the biological kingdom, and you begin to invent a whole universe of new medicines because because you've taken torn out one piece of the encyclopedia, moved it to another animal, moved it to another plant, and begin to manufacture it. Number one, we learned that most human traits are controlled by multiple genes. Um very important thing to realize. Your nose, your eyes, your the color of your hair, your height, um aspects of who you are are controlled by not one gene, typically, but by multiple genes. There are exceptions to this, obviously. And therefore, as a consequence, most common diseases are polygenic. By polygenic, we means multiple genes. So, heart disease, cancer, most common diseases that we encounter, aside from the rare diseases, um are controlled by multiple genes. We should know all of these facts, actually. These are going to be principles that allow us to tackle the questions that I asked you to start with. Many diseases have a genetic links, but right now, we're still in the reading phase. We still don't know the exact genes for most diseases, and we certainly don't know most most genes that govern um fundamental aspects of human normal human traits, height, um hair color, uh eye color, and so forth. This is a very important um um equation. Um I think it's sort of like the equation that governs fundamental laws of physics. The equation that governs fundamental laws of biology is that genes plus environments plus chance gives rise to traits. Most human traits, I mean, really need to understand this. Most human traits are not governed entirely by genes, are not governed entirely by chance, but like by a combination of this. In contrast, some things are highly genetic. So, in some traits, genes will dominate. In some traits, environments will dominate, and occasionally chance will dominate and determine what our ultimate traits are. But, we can ask this question feature by feature, disease by disease, trait by trait. So, in other words, if someone asks me the question, "Is it nature or nurture? Is it gene or environment or chance?" I immediately ask them back the question, "Well, what are we talking about?" Just to give you again classic examples, most of gender anatomy and physiology has a genetic component, whether you are born in Antarctica or whether you're born in um Tanzania, fundamental features of male and female biology for the most part will be governed by genetic components, the anatomy and physiology. There are other aspects of gender identity features, human identity that will be governed by some combination of genes plus environment plus chance. There are aspects of diseases that will be governed by environments and chance, and this will go on over and over again. So, whenever people ask me the question, "Well, is it nature or nurture?" I always say I always ask back, "Well, what feature are we talking about?" because it's going to differ from feature to feature. And we can identify It's not a null question, but it's a question that's very dependent on what we're asking about.
