=== VIDEO: qG_YmIPFO68 (https://youtu.be/qG_YmIPFO68) ===

I want to talk to you
about the future of medicine. But before I do that, I want to talk
a little bit about the past. Now, throughout much
of the recent history of medicine, we've thought about illness and treatment in terms of a profoundly simple model. In fact, the model is so simple that you could summarize it in six words: have disease, take pill, kill something. Now, the reason
for the dominance of this model is of course the antibiotic revolution. Many of you might not know this,
but we happen to be celebrating the hundredth year of the introduction
of antibiotics into the United States. But what you do know is that that introduction
was nothing short of transformative. Here you had a chemical,
either from the natural world or artificially synthesized
in the laboratory, and it would course through your body, it would find its target, lock into its target -- a microbe or some part of a microbe -- and then turn off a lock and a key with exquisite deftness,
exquisite specificity. And you would end up taking
a previously fatal, lethal disease -- a pneumonia, syphilis, tuberculosis -- and transforming that
into a curable, or treatable illness. You have a pneumonia, you take penicillin, you kill the microbe and you cure the disease. So seductive was this idea, so potent the metaphor of lock and key and killing something, that it really swept through biology. It was a transformation like no other. And we've really spent the last 100 years trying to replicate that model
over and over again in noninfectious diseases, in chronic diseases like diabetes
and hypertension and heart disease. And it's worked,
but it's only worked partly. Let me show you. You know, if you take the entire universe of all chemical reactions
in the human body, every chemical reaction
that your body is capable of, most people think that that number
is on the order of a million. Let's call it a million. And now you ask the question, what number or fraction of reactions can actually be targeted by the entire pharmacopoeia,
all of medicinal chemistry? That number is 250. The rest is chemical darkness. In other words, 0.025 percent
of all chemical reactions in your body are actually targetable
by this lock and key mechanism. You know, if you think
about human physiology as a vast global telephone network with interacting nodes
and interacting pieces, then all of our medicinal chemistry is operating on one tiny corner at the edge, the outer edge,
of that network. It's like all of our
pharmaceutical chemistry is a pole operator in Wichita, Kansas who is tinkering with about
10 or 15 telephone lines. So what do we do about this idea? What if we reorganized this approach? In fact, it turns out
that the natural world gives us a sense of how one
might think about illness in a radically different way, rather than disease, medicine, target. In fact, the natural world
is organized hierarchically upwards, not downwards, but upwards, and we begin with a self-regulating,
semi-autonomous unit called a cell. These self-regulating,
semi-autonomous units give rise to self-regulating,
semi-autonomous units called organs, and these organs coalesce
to form things called humans, and these organisms
ultimately live in environments, which are partly self-regulating
and partly semi-autonomous. What's nice about this scheme,
this hierarchical scheme building upwards rather than downwards, is that it allows us
to think about illness as well in a somewhat different way. Take a disease like cancer. Since the 1950s, we've tried rather desperately to apply
this lock and key model to cancer. We've tried to kill cells using a variety of chemotherapies 
or targeted therapies, and as most of us know, that's worked. It's worked for diseases like leukemia. It's worked for some forms
of breast cancer, but eventually you run
to the ceiling of that approach. And it's only in the last 10 years or so that we've begun to think
about using the immune system, remembering that in fact the cancer cell
doesn't grow in a vacuum. It actually grows in a human organism. And could you use the organismal capacity, the fact that human beings
have an immune system, to attack cancer? In fact, it's led to the some of the most
spectacular new medicines in cancer. And finally there's the level
of the environment, isn't there? You know, we don't think of cancer
as altering the environment. But let me give you an example
of a profoundly carcinogenic environment. It's called a prison. You take loneliness, you take depression,
you take confinement, and you add to that, rolled up in a little
white sheet of paper, one of the most potent neurostimulants
that we know, called nicotine, and you add to that one of the most potent
addictive substances that you know, and you have
a pro-carcinogenic environment. But you can have anti-carcinogenic
environments too. There are attempts to create milieus, change the hormonal milieu
for breast cancer, for instance. We're trying to change the metabolic
milieu for other forms of cancer. Or take another disease, like depression. Again, working upwards, since the 1960s and 1970s,
we've tried, again, desperately to turn off molecules
that operate between nerve cells -- serotonin, dopamine -- and tried to cure depression that way, and that's worked,
but then that reached the limit. And we now know that what you
really probably need to do is to change the physiology
of the organ, the brain, rewire it, remodel it, and that, of course,
we know study upon study has shown that talk therapy does exactly that, and study upon study
has shown that talk therapy combined with medicines, pills, really is much more effective
than either one alone. Can we imagine a more immersive
environment that will change depression? Can you lock out the signals
that elicit depression? Again, moving upwards along this
hierarchical chain of organization. What's really at stake perhaps here is not the medicine itself but a metaphor. Rather than killing something, in the case of the great
chronic degenerative diseases -- kidney failure, diabetes,
hypertension, osteoarthritis -- maybe what we really need to do is change
the metaphor to growing something. And that's the key, perhaps, to reframing our thinking about medicine. Now, this idea of changing, of creating a perceptual
shift, as it were, came home to me to roost in a very
personal manner about 10 years ago. About 10 years ago --
I've been a runner most of my life -- I went for a run, a Saturday morning run, I came back and woke up
and I basically couldn't move. My right knee was swollen up, and you could hear that ominous crunch
of bone against bone. And one of the perks of being a physician
is that you get to order your own MRIs. And I had an MRI the next week,
and it looked like that. Essentially, the meniscus of cartilage
that is between bone had been completely torn
and the bone itself had been shattered. Now, if you're looking at me
and feeling sorry, let me tell you a few facts. If I was to take an MRI
of every person in this audience, 60 percent of you would show signs of bone degeneration
and cartilage degeneration like this. 85 percent of all women by the age of 70 would show moderate to severe
cartilage degeneration. 50 to 60 percent
of the men in this audience would also have such signs. So this is a very common disease. Well, the second perk of being a physician is that you can get
to experiment on your own ailments. So about 10 years ago we began, we brought this process
into the laboratory, and we began to do simple experiments, mechanically trying
to fix this degeneration. We tried to inject chemicals
into the knee spaces of animals to try to reverse cartilage degeneration, and to put a short summary
on a very long and painful process, essentially it came to naught. Nothing happened. And then about seven years ago,
we had a research student from Australia. The nice thing about Australians is that they're habitually used to
looking at the world upside down. (Laughter) And so Dan suggested to me, "You know,
maybe it isn't a mechanical problem. Maybe it isn't a chemical problem.
Maybe it's a stem cell problem." In other words, he had two hypotheses. Number one, there is such a thing
as a skeletal stem cell -- a skeletal stem cell that builds up
the entire vertebrate skeleton, bone, cartilage and the fibrous
elements of skeleton, just like there's a stem cell in blood, just like there's a stem cell
in the nervous system. And two, that maybe that, the degeneration
or dysfunction of this stem cell is what's causing osteochondral arthritis,
a very common ailment. So really the question was,
were we looking for a pill when we should have really
been looking for a cell. So we switched our models, and now we began
to look for skeletal stem cells. And to cut again a long story short, about five years ago,
we found these cells. They live inside the skeleton. Here's a schematic and then
a real photograph of one of them. The white stuff is bone, and these red columns that you see
and the yellow cells are cells that have arisen
from one single skeletal stem cell -- columns of cartilage, columns of bone
coming out of a single cell. These cells are fascinating.
They have four properties. Number one is that they live
where they're expected to live. They live just underneath
the surface of the bone, underneath cartilage. You know, in biology,
it's location, location, location. And they move into the appropriate areas
and form bone and cartilage. That's one. Here's an interesting property. You can take them out
of the vertebrate skeleton, you can culture them
in petri dishes in the laboratory, and they are dying to form cartilage. Remember how we couldn't
form cartilage for love or money? These cells are dying to form cartilage. They form their own furls
of cartilage around themselves. They're also, number three, the most efficient repairers
of fractures that we've ever encountered. This is a little bone,
a mouse bone that we fractured and then let it heal by itself. These stem cells have come in
and repaired, in yellow, the bone, in white, the cartilage,
almost completely. So much so that if you label them
with a fluorescent dye you can see them like some kind
of peculiar cellular glue coming into the area of a fracture, fixing it locally
and then stopping their work. Now, the fourth one is the most ominous, and that is that their numbers
decline precipitously, precipitously, tenfold,
fiftyfold, as you age. And so what had happened, really, is that we found ourselves
in a perceptual shift. We had gone hunting for pills but we ended up finding theories. And in some ways we had hooked ourselves
back onto this idea: cells, organisms, environments, because we were now thinking
about bone stem cells, we were thinking about arthritis
in terms of a cellular disease. And then the next question was,
are there organs? Can you build this
as an organ outside the body? Can you implant cartilage
into areas of trauma? And perhaps most interestingly, can you ascend right up
and create environments? You know, we know
that exercise remodels bone, but come on, none of us
is going to exercise. So could you imagine ways of passively
loading and unloading bone so that you can recreate
or regenerate degenerating cartilage? And perhaps more interesting,
and more importantly, the question is, can you apply this model
more globally outside medicine? What's at stake, as I said before,
is not killing something, but growing something. And it raises a series of, I think,
some of the most interesting questions about how we think
about medicine in the future. Could your medicine
be a cell and not a pill? How would we grow these cells? What we would we do to stop
the malignant growth of these cells? We heard about the problems
of unleashing growth. Could we implant
suicide genes into these cells to stop them from growing? Could your medicine be an organ
that's created outside the body and then implanted into the body? Could that stop some of the degeneration? What if the organ needed to have memory? In cases of diseases of the nervous system
some of those organs had memory. How could we implant
those memories back in? Could we store these organs? Would each organ have to be developed
for an individual human being and put back? And perhaps most puzzlingly, could your medicine be an environment? Could you patent an environment? You know, in every culture, shamans have been using
environments as medicines. Could we imagine that for our future? I've talked a lot about models.
I began this talk with models. So let me end with some thoughts
about model building. That's what we do as scientists. You know, when an architect
builds a model, he or she is trying to show you
a world in miniature. But when a scientist is building a model, he or she is trying to show you
the world in metaphor. He or she is trying to create
a new way of seeing. The former is a scale shift.
The latter is a perceptual shift. Now, antibiotics created
such a perceptual shift in our way of thinking about medicine
that it really colored, distorted, very successfully, the way we've thought
about medicine for the last hundred years. But we need new models
to think about medicine in the future. That's what's at stake. You know, there's
a popular trope out there that the reason we haven't had
the transformative impact on the treatment of illness is because we don't have
powerful-enough drugs, and that's partly true. But perhaps the real reason is that we don't have powerful-enough
ways of thinking about medicines. It's certainly true that it would be lovely to have new medicines. But perhaps what's really at stake
are three more intangible M's: mechanisms, models, metaphors. Thank you. (Applause) Chris Anderson:
I really like this metaphor. How does it link in? There's a lot of talk in technologyland about the personalization of medicine, that we have all this data
and that medical treatments of the future will be for you specifically,
your genome, your current context. Does that apply to this model
you've got here? Siddhartha Mukherjee:
It's a very interesting question. We've thought about
personalization of medicine very much in terms of genomics. That's because the gene
is such a dominant metaphor, again, to use that same word,
in medicine today, that we think the genome will drive
the personalization of medicine. But of course the genome
is just the bottom of a long chain of being, as it were. That chain of being, really the first
organized unit of that, is the cell. So, if we are really going to deliver
in medicine in this way, we have to think of personalizing
cellular therapies, and then personalizing
organ or organismal therapies, and ultimately personalizing
immersion therapies for the environment. So I think at every stage, you know -- there's that metaphor,
there's turtles all the way. Well, in this, there's
personalization all the way. CA: So when you say
medicine could be a cell and not a pill, you're talking about
potentially your own cells. SM: Absolutely.
CA: So converted to stem cells, perhaps tested against all kinds
of drugs or something, and prepared. SM: And there's no perhaps.
This is what we're doing. This is what's happening,
and in fact, we're slowly moving, not away from genomics,
but incorporating genomics into what we call multi-order,
semi-autonomous, self-regulating systems, like cells, like organs,
like environments. CA: Thank you so much. SM: Pleasure. Thanks.

It demands it in a way that we can't uh give up at one instance because it is as I describe in my book I call it a red queen's race. You know, there's a very famous scene which I enjoyed from reading uh uh Lewis Carol's books. Um and the Red Queen says uh in the middle Alice runs up to the Red Queen and says, "Why are you running?" And the Red Queen says, "Well, I'm running because the world is moving with equal and opposite ferocity in the other direction. I'm running just to keep in place." Um so cancer demands that kind of running. It demands that kind of running and ultimately it demands that kind of running so that we are not in place but in fact are moving forward. The third article in the same newspaper says, important advanced against breast cancer. And you feel as if there are so many trees that you begin to lose the for what does the forest look like? What does the landscape look like? So I thought I would take a a a bird's eye view as it were and then zoom down on on individual uh changes uh in cancer treatment and prevention. And if you take that bird's eye view, I like to think about cancer in terms of four essentially four revolutions. Uh not a thousand, not 300, but four revolutions. And these four revolutions, we're in the middle of the fourth. uh these four revolutions I think have redefined the way we think about the treatment and prevention of cancer and when we think forward I think we need to go back to reimagine these four structural revolutions as it were in cancer so I'm going to start with my first slide that it's right here um long before that very first revolution arrived uh there was a a long history that predated it. Cancer is thought about as a modern disease, but in fact it is an ancient disease uh probably one of the most ancient diseases we know. This is a picture of u one of the first medical documents that we possess as humans, the Edwin Smith Papyrus, which describes a case of breast cancer. We don't know the words for it because that we we don't have actually the vocabulary, but we can tell from the case description that this was a case of breast cancer. And certainly if you look at ancient specimens of cancer, cancer is present. This is a a a bone tumor found in a mummy uh from from the ancient world in which we know there's a there's a physical presence of cancer in the ancient world. But there was no theory really that explained this for a long time. So the the going back to the drawing board meant going back to a theory again which brings me to the second uh the second idea as it were of cancer uh the second phase of cancer reach the second idea of cancer biology and that is that cancer is a disease not only of cells but of genes. Now remember way back when remember that that that peculiarity that that sand in the eye of cancer theory which was radiation for some reason was able to not only cure cancers but cause cancers and several people over the ne over the over the intervening 30 or 40 years had wondered why is it that radiation they must be doing something to the cell that causes or changing the cell in some way that causes cancer. And in the meantime, several people had figured out that one thing that radiation does is it changes genes. It causes mutations in genes. And it was through that line of thinking, as I said, a sand in the eye of of of cancer theory as a disease of cells that began to illuminate the idea that in fact cancer was of course a disease of cells, but in fact what was really changing were genes. And so there are several actors in this, several players from here from around the around the world. um I won't name all of them but but the essence of the theory was the following that is that the very genes that cause normal cellular growth if you mutate them if you change them for instance by virtue of X-rays you release malignant cellular growth and ultimately that's cancer now we take this idea many of us take this idea for granted now but this was an ex extremely radical idea in the 1970s and 1980s many of you who actually remember the first times uh this idea was announced. It of course was was a revolutionary idea and also a very chilling idea because here we were hoping that in fact it was going to be easy at the genetic level to dissociate cancer from normaly. But what the genetics told us was just the opposite. the genetics was telling us was in fact that the very genes that control normal cellular growth that allow your embryos to grow that allow our hands to grow that allow wounds to be healed that allow stem cells to grow if you mutate those genes you ultimately release abnormal growth abnormal cellular growth and that causes cancer. Now the important consequence of this is that cancer therefore becomes a disease really like nothing else. it becomes a disease in which normaly and illness are twinned like Robert and Elliot Sandler. They're they're part of the same spectrum and that small changes from normaly the very genes that control growth now become uh manifest uh in their malignant form in cancer and this began to explain of course why cancers were caused by environmental influences. This is a picture of PCL pot which one of the first people to figure it out. This is the surgeon general announcing that in fact tobacco causes cancer. If you didn't have a genetic theory of cancer, it'd be hard to explain how things like chemicals in the environment would somehow or the other coax your cells to start dividing abnormally. If you had a genetic theory of cancer, you could now say, well, the real missing link is it's genes plus environment. the environment impinges on genes, changes their structure, changes their it causes mutations and thereby releases cancer or or causes cancer. And that led to the third idea or the third phase of of cancer uh the third general phase of cancer biology, cancer theory, cancer treatment, and that is cancer is a disease not only of cells, not only of genes, but is a disease of genomes. By genomes of course we mean not one gene but the entire complement of about 20,000 odd genes that all human beings possess. So every cell has a complement of about 20 to 25 25,000 genes and cancer is a disease where not one gene is changed but multiple genes have to be changed. So going back to this uh going back to trace this intellectual history uh we we understood that alterations in growth causes causing genes cause cancer. The question is how many how common are they? what are they? What what is their identity? And in fact, several uh studies uh uh indicated that in fact not one one gene was insufficient that you'd have to have many genes. That in fact our cells had natural uh barriers against growth. Growth is a very very fundamentally important process for us. So important that our cells have complicated systems to compute whether they should grow or not grow. The decision for a cell to grow is an incredibly important decision and it requires more than one decision point and it's when many such decision points are abnormally altered by mutations in genes that you ultimately get uh ultimately release a cell that is uh that that can't stop growing or grows abnormally and that of course is cancer. So I want to spend a few minutes here because now we're beginning to come close to our to to to how cancer biologists understand cancer. So we're we're now beginning to circle a very contemporary description of cancer from the scientific point, but it has many many important implications about how we go ahead and what happens next. So here is a a hypothetical picture of a woman who comes into a clinic. Uh let's say she has breast cancer and the blank sheet of paper, forget about the dots for a second. blank sheet of paper has every gene in her body, all the 24,000 genes in her body and the first gene is on the top and you have every gene sort of printed out all the way to the last one and you have the capacity which we do now because of technology invented here and other places. We do now have the capacity to ask the question what genes have changed in her breast cancer cell. Uh what are the alterations of genes? So obviously some genes will be genes that are involved in regulating or computing growth control and you find that you have one two three four five six seven eight genes that have been uh altered in this woman and they're spread out in this kind of landscape view. Now here's the important point which is that the next woman comes in also with breast cancer. If Burkau were to look down his microscope a 100 years ago he would say well both of these are breast cancer. They look identical under the microscope. There's nothing no difference about them. And yet when you look at the genetic anatomy, the genomic anatomy, you find that there are some shared genes that are changed in two breast cancers, but there are some unique genes as well. So every breast cancer is its own breast cancer. And then a third person comes in, a third woman comes in and here too you see some commonalities, but here is another breast cancer which is its own breast cancer. Now you begin to now understand the kind of complexity that cancer biologists are dealing with. They're dealing with a disease whose number one genetic anatomy begs the difference between normal genetic anatomy because the the genes that control growth are very closely allied to normal genes that control growth and number two they're dealing with diversity of a phenomenal level. In other words, every individual specimen of an individual kind of cancer is its own cancer. And that is of course the the the challenge uh for us. That is the challenge. You can you can create uh these things called maps. Uh you know you can take genes that are common and make them into big peaks and genes that are uncommon and make them into small peaks. You can condense this information using uh using complex data algorithms into a picture. And this is actually now you're staring the enemy in the face. This is what the human cancer genome begins to look like. This is from colurectal cancer. Uh this is work work done by Bert Vogglestein. And what you see here are big peaks denoting the very commonly altered genes, medium-sized peaks denoting less commonly altered genes across many specimens. And then of course these dots, these dot, this dot for instance refers to one gene which is unique to one specimen of colorectal cancer not present in any other specimen of colorectal cancer. So the job now therefore is to annotate every single one of these genes and ask the question is it driving the cancer? Is it not driving the cancer? Is it incidental? not incidental. Can there be a medicine directed against this variant and can there not be a medicine against that variant? Now, you could say, "Oh my god, that job is impossible. This is an impossible." The diversity of cancer on one hand and also the fact that normal growth and abnormal growth really depend on the same genetic compliment. Those two tasks make it impossible. But if that was the case, of course, we'd have given up longer. We we we cannot give up. So we have to keep now looking gene by gene and piece of anatomy by piece of anatomy to try to genetic anatomy to try to move forward in trying to treat cancers and in fact it's not all hopeless because in fact you can do that. uh way back even in the 1980s when the first genetic maps were being figured out of cancer there were already attempts uh this is a picture from of an important trial from Japan from China and France which allowed uh the targeting of a of of a particular variant of leukemia acute promyioidic leukemia APML with a single medicine and just to tell you if you find these medicines the effects can be dramatic acute promitic leukemia was the most lethal form of myogynous leukemia ever. So it's like flipping a switch. You literally flipped a switch and all of a sudden a med a disease that was incredibly lethal with a appropriate uh genetic annotation, the appropriate research, the appropriate trials began to be uh uh much more curable disease. Still not fully curable but much more curable disease. And this experience has actually been repeated gene by gene, example by example in other cancers. And yet there remain big ch. We're we're finding out that in truly complex cancers even that won't do. We need to innovate once more again. We to go back to the board and start a a fresh to try to refresh what happens next. So, I'm going to spend the last few minutes talking about my visions and actually my visions that are shared by many people in the cancer field about what happens next. What do we do? What do we do from here? Where does the drawing board go back? To me, what's interesting, what's exciting is the capacity to now incorporate all these pieces of information in a way that's datadriven and then convert that into new treatments, prevention, and potentially cures for some cancer. And it's based on an idea which is now taking root and that is that cancer is not just a disease of cells and genes and genomes but it's an organismal disease. It lives in organism. It lives in real human beings and it it's sustained by physiology that lives inside real human beings. So by organismal I mean it we we think of organisms as having physiology. They have uh they have environments. They have interactions. So can we now think of cancer having physiology as well? What does the physiology of cancer look like? And if that if we can take now not just the genetics, not just this a cellular biology, but if you can think about cancer as a physiological entity, can you now intervene on cancer physiology? Uh organisms live by virtue of complex pathways. They live within complex networks. Well, we need new data and ways to integrate big data into a way that makes sense of the physi physiological networks that sustain cancer. So just to give you one example of this and how data plays into all of this. I mean if you ask someone in the 1980s what cancer how cancer occurred this is this is the time of the genetic theory of cancer they say well there's a mutation it changes a gene that causes cancer. You came back by the 1990s now all of a sudden the mutation sits at the top of this and now you see a network of connected events that allows the cell to be disregulated grow abnormally in a physiological sense and that ultimately causes cancer. And this is now the same pathway in 2014. That's what it looks like now. So you see individual nodes communicating with individual nodes and you say my goodness this gives you a headache. But to biologists it doesn't give us a headache. Actually this is because we want we we need to know instead of building simplified models we need to understand what the level of complexity is because then we can go back and and try to intervene on single networks. So it's not one gene mutated and all of a sudden boom you get an abnormality in growth control. It is that the the the alterations in genomes are causing alterations in cellular physiology. The way cells digest chemicals, the way cells eat sugar, the way cells consume oxygen, the way they interact with the immune system. All of this is happening and ultimately that is what is excuse me unleashing the abnormal growth of cancer cells. And this is uh this is this is true not only at the microscopic level, but it's true at the macroscopic level. This is I'm going to end with this last study which emphasizes that this understanding of cancer in the in in in a physiological sense extends not only to organisms but to networks of organisms to societies. Um this is a study which I find fascinating in which which was which was performed in in Framingham in Massachusetts in which uh and actually went to the study site uh in which they asked the simple question which is that we know that smoking is a is a major cause of lung cancer, lip cancer and other kinds of esophageal cancer etc. And we know we've we spent decades trying to figure out how to stop smoking. There are taxes, there are campaigns against smoking. You watch the television, you watch your and yet smoking persists in a kind of metaphorical analogy in the same way that cancer seems to persist in human society. So can we get rid of of behaviors that that that that cause and and sustain cancers in society? So what they did was very interesting. They went to this uh went to this uh this uh uh village uh a small town in Massachusetts and they said, "Okay, we'll create a social map of every interaction with every other individual." So in other words, if you played golf with Mr. Jones, you would be you'd be connected in this map. Uh if you if your neighbor is another person, that's another connection. Your uncle is in this is this a connection. So it's it's it's Facebook before Facebook. Um uh so they created a social map in this manner. And then they asked a very important question which is that now on top of that social map, the social network, I'm going to now superpose smoking behavior. So every dot here in 1971 corresponds to whether you smoked or not, how much you smoked. uh etc. And then they asked the third question which is now I'm going to follow this network over time. So I'm going to move this network from 1971 to 2000 to ask the question who continues to smoke in despite the the anti-smoking campaigns who quits and who starts. What's very interesting is that in the end the people who continue to smoke versus those who quit really one of the strongest determinants are their neighbors their social neighbors. In other words the social network is an independent determinant determinant. The social physiology is an independent determinant of whether you quit or whether you stay smoking. And so it really begins to make us rethink about addictive behaviors or behaviors in general that are cancer-causing or not not cancer causing. Taxation won't help that necessarily. What you need is you need you need to understand what the structure is, what the physiology is, in this case a macroscopic physiology. And now rethink how can we excuse me infiltrate those networks and disable them in the same way and a microscopic level how you can infiltrate and disable individual cellular networks that cause or sustain cancer. So I'm going to we we've gone on a big journey and I'm going to stop here. The journey begins with cell with cells. It moves to genes. it moves to genomes and ultimately ends in physiology. And it's there's something very satisfying I think in being able to come back to this idea over and over again and emphasizing that we need innovation at every stage and the minute we stop innovating the world will switch and throw us behind it. many ways there was a over promise and under delivery was one of them um uh the suspicion grew even more deeply because um we completely failed to communicate that science is an uncertain um topic that uncertainty is built into science and often what I try to explain to people is that there's a difference between factual uncertainty and methodological uncertainty it's a very very important idea to convey uh factual uncertainty is to say I don't know the result I don't know the answer methodological uncertainty is to say our certainty is to say I don't know the answer but I know how to get to the answer I know there's a as a method by which or a standard or a set of proofs by which I can get to the answer those are different things science has a lot of factual uncertainty many people in this audience who are scientists when you ask them a question will say I don't know but if you ask them how would you know um what would be the criteria of proof um most people in this audience or scientists would say that I I know the answer to that question um so what happened during the covet pandemic is that people mixed up those two things they mixed up what I would call factual uncertainty which was rampant because we didn't know you know what was the direction of the movement what kind of virus was this was this it rosalized what was the effect of the vaccine well decided those are factual uncertainties um they're also a different kind of uncertainty which is a methodological uncertainty what is the standard of proof that is required to call something effective or [Applause] My book is called the emperor of all malades and this and it's a history of cancer and the first question that people ask me is why I wrote the book and I was an onc I am an oncologist I treat cancer patients and I spend most of my life as a researcher. I'm a stem cell scientist. Um, and then I was in training in cancer medicine in Boston when a patient of mine who had an aggressive variant of abdominal cancer and she had responded and relapsed and responded and relapsed. Um, and then she at one point of time she stopped and she said, "Sid, I'm willing to go on with trials and drugs and therapies, but before I do that, I need to know what it is that I'm fighting." And it was a very humbling and a moving moment for me because I realized that not only could I not answer that question as an oncologist, I couldn't point her to a single book or a resource that would answer the question in the fullest sense possible. What it was, what was it that she was fighting? How old was it? Since when has it begun to envelop our lives in the way it does today? Where are we going? What happens next? And and how how did we get here? How do we get in this moment of time with cancer being what it is? And just just so that no one thinks for a second that this is an abstuse problem, I' I'd just like to point out some numbers. I mean, I won't go through all these figures, but just to let you know that there is no doubt statistically that cancer will envelop each and every one of our lives. And if you go to amazon.com or something, you will find 5,000 books about cancer. And for the most part, they're of the variety, oh, you know, eat celery and eat carrots and you'll cure colon cancer. And I began to find these books somewhat insulting actually. So I wrote this book really to answer the question and as I said to fill up a vacuum that existed in this history, in the history of cancer. Um there's a famous saying by uh Hillary who climbed Everest um and when he was asked well why did you climb Everest and he said as you very famously know because it was there and so this book doesn't have any pretensions of being the climb of Mount Everest but really I I wrote this book because it was not there and the fact that it was not there made it impossible for me not to write the book. The book was driven by that urgency the urgency that Andrew and others have felt when cancer envelops our lives. So I can't summarize the entire book. It's a 600page book. I wish you I hope you read it. But I'll tell you a little story about the book and through that story perhaps a story about how the book was written. And in the end I want to spend a few minutes maybe reading a single uh page from the book just to give you a sense of of how such a book is ever written. So my story begins um with the main character in this book Sydney Farber. Um, some of you might have been to Boston, went to the Dana Farber Cancer Institute and wondered who Dana Farber was. There is no Dana Farber. It was Charles Dana and Sydney Farber, two separate individuals. Um, Sydney Farber was a pathologist and uh, he uh, had a tiny basement laboratory at the bottom of Children's Hospital, a freezing uh, about 20T x 14t space uh, full of the smell of formula. And I've been inside that space. it's virtually unlivable. And um he was a pathologist so he essentially only saw the bodies of children. Children would be wheeled down after they were dead into this freezing basement and then he would perform autopsies. But then Sydney Farber had a hunger and it was that hunger actually which drew to to drew him me to him when I first started this book. He began to have this hunger about treating patients. And remember this is 1947 and acute leukemia in children. Acute lymphablastic leukemia was not just a virtually incurable form of cancer. It was a lethal form of cancer in its in its speed. Children would be diagnosed on Monday and would be dead by Friday and two four six year old children. And Farber became very interested in acute lymphablastic leukemia for a single reason and that was that of all the very many forms of cancer it had one advantage and that was that it could be measured and science always begins with measurement and because you could draw blood from a child you could figure out this is the age before cat scans this is before MRI so you can't quantify the growth or the death of an internal tumor but you can quantify the growth growth and death of a blood tumor because you can draw a drop of blood and you can look at it as a pathologist would under microscope. So Farber became obsessed with lymphablastic leukemia and he had a very important clue and that clue came from a very interesting lady Lucy Wills a little bit forgotten in history uh an English hematologist who became interested in finding out uh the cause of various forms of anemia. uh interesting lady, eccentric um she um went to India to investigate a particular form of anemia which no one had seen before or no one had really understood before and this anemia was predominantly present in pregnant women who were malnourished. So she was very interested in why pregnant women who were malnourished they lived in a fac you know these the factory workers had this very peculiar form of anemia and here this is a little note from her um again you see her writing anemia uh pregnancy and then she made a very important discovery she saw that if you fed these women Marmite Marmite she fed these women Marmite the anemia would recover so she became interested in this idea that marmite must have some hidden substance which was responsible for the recovery of the anemia and she didn't eventually figure it out but very soon chemists had broken Marmite down into its various components and of course that substance turned out to be folic acid folate so Wills essentially figured out that deficiencies of folic acid which is a vitamin can cause can block the growth of normal blood and Farber who knew of Will's work began to wonder could an antifolic acid drug block the growth of drug of of a blood cancer. And so Farber had a friend u another uh interesting character who we talk about in the book. He was an Indian chemist lived alone in Boston who had begun to manufacture the series of antifolates. A series of accidents had allowed him to manufacture these antifolates and he began to administer two and four and six year old kids with acute lymphablastic leukemia with antifolates. these antifolates that yellow had yellow subber was the name of the chemist had made. I'm going to tell you a story about how this book is or how a book like this is written because it's the story about the discovery of the first patient who got these antifolates. Um, I became obsessed with finding the child, the first child who got antifolds and had a remission because every time I would feel the pulse of this book, you know, at 600 pages, every time I'd feel the pulse of this book begin to drop, the my instinct was always to find the patient. Find the patients and their stories will finish the story of the book for you. So, I began to be obsessed with finding the patient who had and I knew only the following piece of information. So, so um so there she is, Mary Lasker. Mary Laskan Farber teamed up and she and Farber began to put pressure on the nation, particularly on Nixon. And this advertisement appeared in 1969. And this really marks the beginning of the war on cancer. And so Mr. Nixon, you can cure cancer. And there's a subtext there which says, wouldn't it be very nice to cure cancer? Cure cancer by America's 200th birthday. So that birthday, as you know, has gone and passed. And where are we today? Now before we go on, let me spend one second reminding you that this is the 1950s and 1960s. America is about to land a man on the moon. Um the Manhattan project which is a long inheritance has been phenomenally successful. So imagine the hubris of this minute of this moment. Um here are Farber and Mary Alaska who want to cure cancer by America's 200th birthday. And as someone said it to me that is almost like landing a rocket on the moon without knowing Newton's laws. But there they were and we have to be thankful to them for it. Uh what happens next is that there was an enormous expansion in the budget of the National Cancer Institute and we see this rising up from the 1970 onward. That arrow marks the uh that arrow marks the moment that the uh war on cancer was launched and the question you might ask yourself is well what happened and the answer is for a long time not very much happened. This is this next slide shows the uh cancer related deaths if uh divided in various age ranges between 1955 and 2004. And the first thing that you see is that between 1970 odd to 1980 give or take a few years the death rate was static for most age groups and if anything the death rate was rising but then things started turning around and in the last part of the book I talk about what happens next. And one of the things that's happened next is visible in this slide. So this is a a slide that plots the total cancer mortality in in the US over several decades bringing us actually right up to 2006 in this case divided into men and women. And first thing you notice of course is the cancer rates are the cancer deaths are rising. This is largely because of the aging population overall and because the population is increasing. So this is a natural rise. But what's very gratifying of course is that you find that about 1990s 2000s you see this plateauing effect. Now importantly that plateauing effect hasn't turned around and one of the great challenges of our generation is to turn that plateau of cancer around. Now h how are we going to do this? Uh I don't know but there has been an explosion in understanding in the understanding of the cancer cell. So if you return back to my original analogy now we finally know Newton's laws. Now that doesn't mean that we can land a rocket on the moon but the knowledge of even the glimpse of Newton's laws makes a huge difference. This is a slide that I borrowed from Bert Voggelstein very important cancer researcher which talks about the mutations in the cancer genome. This is called the landscape of the cancer genome. I'd like us to get very familiar with this landscape because this is going to haunt us in our lives. So here's here's a way that Rogelstein depicts it. He's basically put every gene on the sheet of paper and he's marked out for a given individual cancer. He's marked out every mutation in a white dot and in these peaks and the red peaks and mountains are those white dots across a 100 or 200 odd samples from the same cancer. And what we learned from this slide? We learned something very important. Number one, we learned that there are there is an overlap for a single cancer between one dot and the peak, which means that in a single form of in a single cancer in a single colurectal cancer, there is an overlap between its mutation and the most common mutations across a 100 samples. But we also learned something very troubling and that is if you look there are many white dots that do not correspond to peaks and that means every single colurectal cancer this is not even I'm not talking across different cancers every single colurectal cancer therefore contains a heterogeneity of mutations and again I point this out using some examples right here. So should we be dejected and stop? No, because it turns out that some mutations in cancers are more important than others. And some mutations, if you target them with the appropriate kind of drug, you can get uh an amazing clinical response. And again, I was obsessed in this book at finding the first patients. And so here is Barbara Bradfield. She actually sent me this picture. Barbara Bradfield is the longest survivor of her septin treatment. She is an iconic patient. She's someone that history will remember because she gave up her time and her potentially her life to enroll in the first trial of herception. She's still alive today. She had metastatic breast cancer. Um so in conclusion, what does this book tell us? This book tells us that we are in in this this battle for a very long time, but an enormous amount of resources are going to be deployed in order to battle this very elemental and magisterial illness. So I'm going to now turn and just read you uh the very last page of my book. Um and again uh as a reminder of what lies ahead perhaps. Uh this is the story of Germaine. I've changed the names and dates of the patients in this book uh to protect uh privacy. But this is the story of Germaine, a woman who had relapsed many many times uh with abdominal cancer. had an had an incredible response to a particular drug and had stretched out a relapse sorry response for about six and a half years and then finally relapsed again. Uh when I recall that final conversation with Germaine, embarrassingly enough the object seemed to stand out more vividly than the words. A hospital room with its sharp smell of disinfectant and hand soap. The steely unflattering overhead light. a wooden side table on wheels piled with pills, books, newspaper clippings, nail polish, jewelry, postcards. Her room wallpapered with pictures of her beautiful house in Montgomery and of her daughter holding some fruit picked from her garden. A standard issue plastic hospital picture filled with a bunch of sunflowers perched on a table by their side. Germaine, as I remember her, was sitting by the bed, one leg dangling casually down, wearing her usual eccentric and arresting combination of clothes and some large and unusual pieces of jewelry. Her hair was carefully arranged. She looked formal, frozen, and perfect, like a photograph of someone in the hospital waiting to die. She seemed content. She laughed. She joked. She made wearing a nasogastric tube seem effortless and somehow dignified. Only years later in writing this book could I finally put into words why that meeting left me so uneasy and humbled. Why the gestures in that room seemed larger than life. Why the object seemed like symbols. Why Germaine herself seemed like an actor playing a part. Nothing I realized was incidental. The characteristics of Germaine's personality that had once seemed spontaneous and impulsive were in fact calculated and almost reflexive responses to her illness. Her clothes were loose and vivid because they were decoys against the growing outline of the tumor in her abdomen. Her necklace was distractingly large so as to pull attention away from her cancer. Her room was topsyturvy with bobbles and pictures. The hospital flacked to the wall because without them it would devolve into the cold anonymity of any other room in any other hospital. She had dangled her leg at that precise posed angle because the tumor had invaded her spine and begun to paralyze her other leg, making it impossible to sit any other way. Her her casualness was studied. The jokes were rehearsed. Her illness had tried to humiliate her. It had made her anonymous and seemingly humorless. It had sentenced her to die in unsightly death in a freezing hospital room thousands of miles away from home. But she had responded with vengeance, moving always to be one step ahead, trying to outwit it. It was like watching someone locked in a chess game. Every time Germaine's disease moved, imposing yet another terrifying constraint on her, she made an equally assertive movement in turn. The illness acted, she reacted. It was a morbid hypnotic game, a game that had taken over her life. She dodged one blow only to be caught by the other. She too was like Carol's Red Queen, stuck peddling furiously just to keep in the same place. Germaine seemed that evening to have captured something essential about our struggle against cancer. That to keep keep pace with this malady, you need to keep inventing and reinventing, learning and unlearning strategies. Germaine fought cancer obsessively, cannilally, desperately, fiercely, madly, brilliantly, and zealously, as if channeling all the fierce inventive energy of generations of men and women who would who had fought cancer in the past and would fight it in the future. Her quest for a cure had taken her on a strange and limitless journey through internet blogs and teaching hospitals and chemotherapy and clinical trials halfway across the country through a landscape more desolate, desperate, and disquing than she had imagined. She had deployed every last morsel of her energy to the quest, mobilizing and remobilizing the last dregs of her courage, summoning her will and wit and imagination until that final evening she had stared into the vault of her resourcefulness and found it empty. In that haunted last night, hanging on to her life by no more than a tenuous thread, summoning all her strength and dignity as she wheeled herself to the privacy of her bathroom. It was as if she'd encapsulated the essence of a 4,000-year-old war. Thank you.

book because you then become unreadable. It's an unreadable book. You produce a document that no one
will read and therefore has really no consequence in, in, in
contributing to the conversation. So you have to make choices and be ready
to be critiqued by people about the sources. People are disappointed in those
choices. Um, but you have, you know, as a public figure, as a writer,

Well, I loved your first book, The Emperor of All Malades. I was excited to see you found time to write another one. The first book, there's a long sort of history of trying to understand cancer. But this book, Gene, is more about the future than than 50% of this book is in the future. What happens now that that the human genome has become mostly readable? The central question in this book is, you know, when a machine starts to understand its own code of instructions, what happens to that machine? Does it change its code of instructions? Does it learn to limit those changes? Is that limiting of the instructions part of the instructions themselves? That's the central sort of animous of this book. Yeah. To me, that's such a fascinating question because it would almost seem cruel not to fix cystic fibrosis or a variety of even more rare diseases. I think these are powerful technologies and dangerous technologies and the conversation has to be quite wide. But now the technologies are getting to the place where that's going to become technologically possible I would imagine in the next 10 odd years. Most of the stuff you hear about is is pretty much on the side that you consider positive like the disease type editing for our patients. I can see a road map in which the use of gene editing technologies could have therapeutic implications. One that's of interest to our foundation is HIV. Although a very tough one that's probably pretty far off is whether you could edit people's immune cells so that they're cured of HIV. Again, the questions are scalability, you know, how expensive is it going to be? And the perennial question, what are the offtarget effects? Are there unintended consequences that we don't know yet? We will know at least for some cohort of of human beings as early as 2 to 3 years. And what percentage of your cancer patients now would have some type of sequencing done to to help guide their treatment? So for blood cancer, I would say some type of sequencing. I would say it's coming up to about 89%. And that's because in some cases you'll actually change the treatment, which drugs or how you go about it based on that data. That's right. That field is in its infancy. Um, and I think there's been a lot of hype around that field. Being able to use genetic and genomic information from a tumor and using that to understand therapy or to direct therapy, I think is an idea which has deep biological justification. It hasn't come about yet because the technologies aren't there yet. So, we shouldn't be selling the idea as it were. Uh, it's a research project, but it's a very valid research project. It's one of the most valid research projects in cancer. We need all our skills, all our wits to understand what happens once we start changing or beginning to change the instructions with which were built.
